paper_authors: Allen, M. W.; Baines, S.
paper_title: Manipulating the symbolic meaning of meat to encourage greater acceptance of fruits and vegetables and less proclivity for red and white meat
intervention_description: Participants were informed of a prior scientific study indicating that individuals who endorse social hierarchy and human dominance over nature consume higher quantities of red and white meat, whereas those who reject these structures consume more fruits and vegetables. This intervention aimed to increase the salience of meat’s symbolic meaning.
intervention_sample_description: residents of a moderate size city in Australia
control_sample_description: residents of a moderate size city in Australia
outcome_description: Percentage of participants who reduced red meat consumption

paper_authors: Allen, M. W.; Baines, S.
paper_title: Manipulating the symbolic meaning of meat to encourage greater acceptance of fruits and vegetables and less proclivity for red and white meat
intervention_description: Participants were informed of a prior scientific study indicating that individuals who endorse social hierarchy and human dominance over nature consume higher quantities of red and white meat, whereas those who reject these structures consume more fruits and vegetables. This intervention aimed to increase the salience of meat’s symbolic meaning.
intervention_sample_description: residents of a moderate size city in Australia
control_sample_description: residents of a moderate size city in Australia
outcome_description: Percentage of participants who reduced white meat consumption

paper_authors: Amiot, C. E.; El Hajj Boutros, G.; Sukhanova, K.; Karelis, A. D.
paper_title: Testing a novel multicomponent intervention to reduce meat consumption in young men
intervention_mechanism: info: health, info: environment, info: animal welfare, norms: dynamic, perspective taking/individuation, goal pursuit: planning/pledge/reminder
intervention_description: Participants received a presentation on declining meat consumption trends in Canada since 1980 and the negative health, animal welfare, and environmental impacts of meat consumption (including PETA videos). They completed a mind attribution task writing about a cow's thoughts and feelings, received practical tips for planning meat-free meals and making substitutions, and set a monthly goal to reduce, maintain, or increase their meat consumption.
intervention_sample_description: university students
control_description: Participants received information about how to complete dietary journals and were instructed to keep a record of their food intake, including condiments and beverages, over two weekdays and one weekend day; they were encouraged to write as much as possible. They were provided with food weight scales to estimate their portion sizes, and the first dietary journal was completed in the following week, representing a baseline measure of meat eating.
control_sample_description: university students
outcome_description: Total meat consumption over two weekdays and one weekend day

paper_authors: Anderson, J.
paper_title: An experimental investigation of the impact of video media on pork consumption
intervention_description: Participants watched a video about pigs in animal agriculture on a tablet.
intervention_sample_description: presumably mostly university students from the US east and west coast
control_description: Control participants were not exposed to a video, but it was not a truly neutral control condition, in that the canopy used for the study was covered in graphic images of farm animals.
control_sample_description: presumably mostly university students from the US east and west coast
outcome_description: Frequency of pork consumption over the past 30 days

paper_authors: Anderson, J.
paper_title: An experimental investigation of the impact of video media on pork consumption
intervention_description: Participants watched a 360 degrees video about pigs in animal agriculture. They wore Samsung headsets, which allow viewers to pan in any direction for the duration of each frame.
intervention_sample_description: presumably mostly university students from the US east and west coast
control_description: Control participants were not exposed to a video, but it was not a truly neutral control condition, in that the canopy used for the study was covered in graphic images of farm animals.
control_sample_description: presumably mostly university students from the US east and west coast
outcome_description: Frequency of pork consumption over the past 30 days

paper_authors: Anderson, E. C.; Feldman Barrett, L.
paper_title: Affective beliefs influence the experience of eating meat
intervention_mechanism: origin of animal product, info: animal welfare
intervention_description: Participants tasted a ham sample which had a description about the origin farm of the ham: "This ham was produced at a factory farm that focused on production. The animals were confined to concrete indoor pens where they were unable to lie down or go outside. The animals were isolated so could not engage in social behaviors with other pigs."
intervention_sample_description: Northeastern undergraduate students
control_sample_description: Northeastern undergraduate students
outcome_description: Likelihood of eating sampled pork again

paper_authors: Bertolaso, C.
paper_title: Investigating the effectiveness of message framing and regulatory fit in increasing positive animal attitude and reducing animal products consumption. a study for animal equality
intervention_mechanism: info: animal welfare, goal pursuit: efficacy/consequences/feedback
intervention_description: Facebook posts showing what farmed animals endure in factory farms. Promotion-focused framing of the outcome variable (e.g., "each vegan saves 200 animals per year").
intervention_sample_description: English-speaking non-vegan followers of Animal Equality
control_sample_description: English-speaking non-vegan followers of Animal Equality
outcome_description: Frequency of animal product consumption (poultry, fish, beef, pork, eggs, dairy) during the past week

paper_authors: Bertolaso, C.
paper_title: Investigating the effectiveness of message framing and regulatory fit in increasing positive animal attitude and reducing animal products consumption. a study for animal equality
intervention_mechanism: info: animal welfare, goal pursuit: efficacy/consequences/feedback
intervention_description: Facebook posts showing what farmed animals endure in factory farms. Prevention-focused framing of the outcome variable (e.g., "each vegan prevents 270 animals per year from being killed").
intervention_sample_description: English-speaking non-vegan followers of Animal Equality
control_sample_description: English-speaking non-vegan followers of Animal Equality
outcome_description: Frequency of animal product consumption (poultry, fish, beef, pork, eggs, dairy) during the past week

paper_authors: Bertolaso, C.
paper_title: Investigating the effectiveness of message framing and regulatory fit in increasing positive animal attitude and reducing animal products consumption. a study for animal equality
intervention_mechanism: info: animal welfare, goal pursuit: efficacy/consequences/feedback, perspective taking/individuation
intervention_description: Facebook posts showing the special characteristics of animals presented as unique individuals. Promotion-focused framing of the outcome variable (e.g., "each vegan saves 200 animals per year").

control_sample_description: English-speaking non-vegan followers of Animal Equality

paper_authors: Byrd-Bredbenner, C.; Grenci, A.; Quick, V.
paper_title: Effect of a television programme on nutrition cognitions and intended behaviours
intervention_mechanism: origin of animal product, authority/role models
intervention_description: Participants watched the "Lisa the vegetarian" Simpson's episode. Lisa visits a petting zoo and bonds with a lamb, which makes her unable to eat lamb chops at dinner that night. She then decides to give up meat entirely which triggers several conflicts at school and at home. Lisa finds unexpected allies in Apu and Paul and Linda McCartney, who happen to be at the Kwik-E-Mart. They encourage Lisa but also gently push back: she shouldn't try to force her beliefs on others.
intervention_sample_description: young girls (9-10 years-old) from NJ, US
control_sample_description: young girls (9-10 years-old) from NJ, US
outcome_description: Average of three meat attitude items (e.g., there is nothing wrong with eating meat)

paper_authors: Byrd-Bredbenner, C.; Grenci, A.; Quick, V.
paper_title: Effect of a television programme on nutrition cognitions and intended behaviours
intervention_mechanism: origin of animal product, authority/role models
intervention_description: Participants watched the "Lisa the vegetarian" Simpson's episode. intervention_sample_description: young girls (9-10 years-old) from NJ, US
control_sample_description: young girls (9-10 years-old) from NJ, US
outcome_description: Average of three intended behavior items (e.g., I plan to become a vegetarian)

paper_authors: Campbell-Arvai, V.; Arvai, J.; Kalof, L.
paper_title: Motivating sustainable food choices: The role of nudges, value orientation, and information provision
intervention_description: Participants received a menu listing only five appealing meat-free options. Participants were informed—verbally and in writing on the menu—that they could also consult a second menu with an array of popular non-vegetarian dining hall dishes that was posted on the wall approximately 3.5 m away from their table.
intervention_sample_description: undergraduate students at a Midwestern university
control_description: Participants received a menu in which five appealing meat-free options were presented on the same menu as the meat options. The leaf symbol and accompanying text were not present, and thus meat-free options were not differentiated from those menu items containing meat.
control_sample_description: undergraduate students at a Midwestern university
outcome_description: Percentage of participants choosing a non-meat option

paper_authors: Campbell-Arvai, V.; Arvai, J.; Kalof, L.
paper_title: Motivating sustainable food choices: The role of nudges, value orientation, and information provision
intervention_description: Participants received a menu in which appealing meat-free options were presented on the same menu as the meat options but the meat-free options were differentiated by a stylized leaf symbol and accompanying text printed at the bottom of menu: "Recent scientific studies have suggested that consuming less meat can help to reduce our environmental impact."
intervention_sample_description: undergraduate students at a Midwestern university
control_description: Participants received a menu in which five appealing meat-free options were presented on the same menu as the meat options. The leaf symbol and accompanying text were not present, and thus meat-free options were not differentiated from those menu items containing meat.
control_sample_description: undergraduate students at a Midwestern university
outcome_description: Percentage of participants choosing a non-meat option

paper_authors: Carfora, V.; Caso, D.; Conner, M.
paper_title: Correlational study and randomised controlled trial for understanding and changing red meat consumption: the role of eating identities
intervention_mechanism: info: health, info: taste/disgust, goal pursuit: planning/pledge/reminder
intervention_description: At the beginning of the experimental phase, e-mails clarified that the recommended intake of red meat is at most two medium portions per week (200 g). Participants received daily SMS text messages to remind them to self-monitor their red meat consumption using a food diary, and not exceed 200g per week.
intervention_sample_description: italian undergraduate students
control_sample_description: italian undergraduate students
outcome_description: Average agreement with three intention items (e.g., I intend to eat less than two medium portions of red meat over the next week)

paper_authors: Carfora, V.; Caso, D.; Conner, M.
paper_title: Correlational study and randomised controlled trial for understanding and changing red meat consumption: the role of eating identities

intervention_sample_description: italian undergraduate students
control_sample_description: italian undergraduate students
outcome_description: Average agreement with three affective attitude items (e.g., eating less than two medium portion of red meat in the next weeks is unenjoyable - enjoyable)

paper_authors: Carfora, V.; Caso, D.; Conner, M.
paper_title: Randomised controlled trial of a text messaging intervention for reducing processed meat consumption: the mediating roles of anticipated regret and intention
intervention_mechanism: info: health, emotions: negative, info: taste/disgust, goal pursuit: planning/pledge/reminder
intervention_description: Participants were informed they would receive an e-mail, in which was clarified that the recommended PMC is at most one small portion per week (50g), and that they would daily receive for 1 week a SMS on WhatsApp. This message focused on anticipated regret that urged participants to self-monitor processed meat consumption.
intervention_sample_description: italian undergraduate students
control_sample_description: italian undergraduate students
outcome_description: Average agreement with three intention items (e.g., in the next week, I intend to not eat more than one portion of processed meat [50 g])

paper_authors: Cooney, N.
paper_title: What elements make a vegetarian leaflet more effective?
intervention_mechanism: authority/role models, info: health, info: animal welfare, goal pursuit: planning/pledge/reminder, norms: dynamic
intervention_description: Participants received a booklet focusing primarily on cruelty, discussing all farm animals, and why to go vegetarian. Health considerations were also mentioned. No time constraints were imposed; participants could read the booklet for as long or as short a period as they preferred.
intervention_sample_description: young individuals at the Warped Tour Music Festival in a variety of cities in the northeastern U.S and students at colleges and universities in the Boston, Philadelphia, and Dallas regions.
control_sample_description: young individuals at the Warped Tour Music Festival in a variety of cities in the northeastern U.S and students at colleges and universities in the Boston, Philadelphia, and Dallas regions.
outcome_description: Number of meals in a typical week that include beef or pork

paper_authors: Rocha Possidónio da Silva, C.
paper_title: Am I too cute to eat? The effect of cuteness appeal on the promotion of a more plant-based diet
intervention_description: Participants were exposed to a picture portraying a high-cuteness pig together with three different pork-meals.
intervention_sample_description: predominantly urban dwelling Portuguese-speaking omnivores
control_description: Participants were only exposed to a white square - frame with the same size of pictures presented on both experimental conditions to equalize conditions.
control_sample_description: predominantly urban dwelling Portuguese-speaking omnivores
outcome_description: Valence of the pork meal shown in an image

intervention_description: Participants were exposed to a picture portraying a low-cuteness pig together with three different pork-meals.
intervention_sample_description: predominantly urban dwelling Portuguese-speaking omnivores
control_description: Participants were only exposed to a white square - frame with the same size of pictures presented on both experimental conditions to equalize conditions.
control_sample_description: predominantly urban dwelling Portuguese-speaking omnivores
outcome_label: willingness to follow a plant-based diet
outcome_description: Willingness to follow a plant-based diet

paper_authors: Fehrenbach, K. S.
paper_title: Designing messages to reduce meat consumption: A test of the extended parallel process model
intervention_description: Participants watched a 7-minute video that informed viewers of the negative health effects of high meat consumption (high threat) and suggested easy ways to reduce their meat consumption to increase perceived efficacy.
intervention_sample_description: US residents who consume meat at least 7 times per week
control_description: The control group did not watch the video.
control_sample_description: US residents who consume meat at least 7 times per week
outcome_description: Intention to eat less meat in the next seven days

paper_authors: Fehrenbach, K. S.
paper_title: Designing messages to reduce meat consumption: A test of the extended parallel process model
intervention_description: Participants watched a 4-minute video containing the same information about the negative health effects of high meat consumption (high threat) along with the recommended action to eat less meat, but it only included a very minor efficacy component in the conclusion.
intervention_sample_description: US residents who consume meat at least 7 times per week
control_description: The control group did not watch the video.
control_sample_description: US residents who consume meat at least 7 times per week
outcome_description: Intention to eat less meat in the next seven days

control_sample_description: US residents who consume meat at least 7 times per week
outcome_description: Average attitude towards changing diet based on seven dimensions (e.g., bad/good, negative/positive, harmful/beneficial)

paper_authors: Flens, G.; Moleman, P.; de Rooy, L.
paper_title: The effectiveness of leafletting on reducing the consumption of animal products in Dutch students
intervention_mechanism: info: health, info: animal welfare, authority/role models, norms: dynamic, perspective taking/individuation, goal pursuit: planning/pledge/reminder
intervention_description: Participants received a leaflet "​Maak het Verschil"​, a Dutch translation of a highly popular leaflet distributed by Vegan Outreach, Compassionate Choices. The content of the leaflet were adjusted to fit with the local situation in the Netherlands.

control_description: Participants did not receive the leaflet.
control_sample_description: Dutch students
outcome_description: Change in chicken consumption in the last 3 months

paper_authors: Loy, L. S.; Wieber, F.; Gollwitzer, P. M.; Oettingen, G.
paper_title: Supporting sustainable food consumption: Mental contrasting with implementation intentions (MCII) aligns intentions and behavior
intervention_mechanism: info: health, info: environment, info: animal welfare
intervention_description: Participants read a text providing information about meat consumption which was drawing on an article and a book written by a renowned German nutritionist. The text adressed environmental consequences as well as other arguments for reduced consumption. These arguments included ethical, health, social/political aspects of meat consumption including references to scientific publications for reduced consumption.
intervention_sample_description: participants from a German university
control_sample_description: participants from a German university
outcome_description: Meat consumption in grams per day.

paper_authors: Loy, L. S.; Wieber, F.; Gollwitzer, P. M.; Oettingen, G.
paper_title: Supporting sustainable food consumption: Mental contrasting with implementation intentions (MCII) aligns intentions and behavior
intervention_mechanism: info: health, info: environment, info: animal welfare, goal pursuit: planning/pledge/reminder
intervention_description: Participants read a text providing information about meat consumption which was drawing on an article and a book written by a renowned German nutritionist. The text adressed environmental consequences as well as other arguments for reduced consumption. These arguments included ethical, health, social/political aspects of meat consumption including references to scientific publications for reduced consumption. Participants completed a procedure for mental contrasting with implementation intentions in which individuals wrote down (1) a personal goal, (2) positive outcomes of goal achievement, the best outcome, and respective associations, (3) obstacles of goal achievement, their two main obstacles, and respective associations, and finally (4) four IIs addressing their two main obstacles.
intervention_sample_description: participants from a German university
control_sample_description: participants from a German university
outcome_description: Meat consumption in grams per day.

paper_authors: Novotná, A.
paper_title: The influence of movie on behavioral change in individual meat and dairy products consumption
intervention_description: Participants watched "Babe: The gallant pig", a family comedy movie from 1996 about a pig who wants to become a sheepdog.
intervention_sample_description: Czech adults
control_description: Participants for the control group were recruited via the Facebook events of the movie screenings with a message that encouraged anybody who couldn’t attend the screenings but still wanted to join the experiment to fill in the survey.
control_sample_description: Czech adults
outcome_description: Frequency of beef consumption

paper_authors: Novotná, A.
paper_title: The influence of movie on behavioral change in individual meat and dairy products consumption
intervention_mechanism: info: animal welfare, logical argument, perspective taking/individuation

paper_authors: Palomo-Vélez, G.; Tybur, J. M.; van Vugt, M.
paper_title: Unsustainable, unhealthy, or disgusting? Comparing different persuasive messages against meat consumption
intervention_description: Participants read a persuasive essay describing disgust-evoking aspects of meat production and consumption (e.g. "...ABC News recently broke a story higlighting the amount of "pink slime" that is added to the ground beef that you see in grocery stores, restaurants, and even school cafeterias...").
intervention_sample_description: US residents
control_description: Participants read a neutral essay about the 1980 Winter Olympics.
control_sample_description: US residents
outcome_label: purchasing intentions for five meat items
outcome_description: Likelihood of buying meat items shown in photos

paper_authors: Palomo-Vélez, G.; Tybur, J. M.; van Vugt, M.
paper_title: Unsustainable, unhealthy, or disgusting? Comparing different persuasive messages against meat consumption
intervention_description: Participants read a persuasive essay describing health issues of meat consumption (e.g. "...A building body of research indicates that our meat consumption has led to a sharp increase in chronic disease and, for many people, to an early grave...").
intervention_sample_description: US residents
control_description: Participants read a neutral essay about the 1980 Winter Olympics.
control_sample_description: US residents
outcome_label: purchasing intentions for five meat items
outcome_description: Likelihood of buying meat items shown in photos

paper_authors: Palomo-Vélez, G.; Tybur, J. M.; van Vugt, M.
paper_title: Unsustainable, unhealthy, or disgusting? Comparing different persuasive messages against meat consumption
intervention_description: Participants read a persuasive essay describing the moral (environmental and animal welfare) issues of meat consumption (e.g. "...Up to 10 distressed and suffering birds are shoved into a single small cage, in one huge shed with up to 90.000 other chickens..." and "...Increases in meat prodcution have led to incredible stain on our natural resources; in fact, we currently use one-third of the world's fresh water and one-third of total arable land just to raise livestock...").
intervention_sample_description: US residents
control_description: Participants read a neutral essay about the 1980 Winter Olympics.
control_sample_description: US residents
outcome_label: purchasing intentions for five meat items
outcome_description: Likelihood of buying meat items shown in photos

paper_authors: Sparkman, G.; Walton, G. M.
paper_title: Dynamic norms promote sustainable behavior, even if it is counternormative
intervention_description: Partcipants saw the message: "Recent research has shown that, in the last 5 years, 30% of Americans have now started to make an effort to limit their meat consumption. That means that, in recent years, 3 in 10 people have changed their behavior and begun to eat less meat than they otherwise would."
intervention_sample_description: US adults
control_description: Participants were not given a norm statement (message).
control_sample_description: US adults
outcome_description: Interest in eating less meat

paper_authors: Sparkman, G.; Walton, G. M.
paper_title: Dynamic norms promote sustainable behavior, even if it is counternormative
intervention_description: Partcipants saw the message: "Recent research has shown that 30% of Americans make an effort to limit their meat consumption. That means that 3 in 10 people eat less meat than they otherwise would."
intervention_sample_description: US adults
control_description: Participants were not given a norm statement (message).
control_sample_description: US adults
outcome_description: Interest in eating less meat

paper_authors: Sparkman, G.; Walton, G. M.
paper_title: Dynamic norms promote sustainable behavior, even if it is counternormative
intervention_description: Partcipants saw the message: "Some people are starting to limit how much meat they eat. This is true both nationally and here at Stanford. Specifically, recent research has shown that, over the last 5 years, 30% of Americans have started to make an effort to limit their meat consumption. That means that, in recent years, 3 in 10 people have changed their behavior and begun to eat less meat than they otherwise would."
intervention_sample_description: customers waiting in line at a café on the Stanford campus
control_sample_description: customers waiting in line at a café on the Stanford campus
outcome_description: Percentage of purchased meals containing meat

paper_authors: Sparkman, G.; Walton, G. M.
paper_title: Dynamic norms promote sustainable behavior, even if it is counternormative
intervention_description: Partcipants saw the message: "Some people limit how much meat they eat. This is true both nationally and here at Stanford. Specifically, recent research has shown that 30% of Americans make an effort to limit their meat consumption. That means that 3 in 10 people eat less meat than they otherwise would."
intervention_sample_description: customers waiting in line at a café on the Stanford campus
control_sample_description: customers waiting in line at a café on the Stanford campus
outcome_description: Percentage of purchased meals containing meat

paper_authors: Tian, Q.; Hilton, D.; Becker, M.
paper_title: Confronting the meat paradox in different cultural contexts: Reactions among Chinese and French participants
intervention_description: Participants saw a photo of a cow with a short statement saying that the cow will be sent to the abattoir tomorrow. This condition aimed to build a strong connection between meat and animals.
intervention_sample_description: French residents
control_sample_description: French residents
outcome_description: Average of desire to eat beef in the next days and intention to reduce beef consumption during the coming year

paper_authors: Zickfeld, J. H.; Kunst, J. R.; Hohle, S. M.
paper_title: Too sweet to eat: Exploring the effects of cuteness on meat consumption
intervention_description: Participants were shown the same lamb chop advertisement used in the control condition; however, this version also included an image of a cute lamb.
intervention_sample_description: US adults
control_description: Participants were shown an advertisement for lamb chops.
control_sample_description: US adults
outcome_description: Feelings about eating meat

paper_authors: Animal Charity Evaluators
paper_title: 2013 humane education study
intervention_mechanism: info: health, info: environment, info: animal welfare, info: taste/disgust
intervention_description: Participants attended a presentation that documents the abuse on factory farms, is pro animal, and asked students to reduce or eliminate animal meat and products from their diets. The focus was mainly on the ethical implications of factory farming, and also touched on health and environmental consequences.
intervention_sample_description: high school and college students
control_description: Participants from a different class that did not attend the presentation.
control_sample_description: high school and college students
outcome_description: Frequency of red meat consumption in the last 7 days

paper_authors: Animal Charity Evaluators
paper_title: 2013 leafleting study
intervention_mechanism: perspective taking/individuation, info: animal welfare, info: health, authority/role models, goal pursuit: planning/pledge/reminder, norms: dynamic
intervention_description: Participants were offered and took one of two Vegan Outreach: "Compassionate Choices" or "Even If You Like Meat". These leaflets were substantially similar, and are intended to promote veganism, vegetarianism, and reduced consumption of animal products.
intervention_sample_description: college and university students
control_description: Participants were offered and took a control flyer on puppy mills called "The Cruelty Behind Cuteness". Since this flyer portrays animal suffering in puppy mills and is not expected to cause dietary change, it was distributed as the control leaflet.
control_sample_description: college and university students
outcome_description: Frequency of red meat consumption in the last 7 days

paper_authors: Schwitzgebel, E.; Cokelet, B.; Singer, P.
paper_title: Do ethics classes influence student behavior? Case study: Teaching the ethics of eating meat
intervention_description: Participants read James Rachels' article “The Basic Argument for Vegetarianism”, an introductory-level ten-page philosophy article arguing that it is unethical to eat meat. Participants were also encouraged to view, on their own outside of class, an optional eleven-minute vegetarianism advocacy video “What Came Before”.
intervention_sample_description: students enrolled in four introductory Philosophy courses at University of California, Riverside
control_description: Participants read Peter Singer's article “The Singer Solution to World Poverty”, a six-page opinion article that had originally appeared in New York Times Magazine arguing unethical aspect of luxury spending instead of making donations to charity. Participants were also encouraged to view, on their own outside of class, an optional nineteen-minute video advocating donation to hunger-relief initiatives: “Ending Hunger Now”.
control_sample_description: students enrolled in four introductory Philosophy courses at University of California, Riverside
outcome_description: Percentage of participants buying a meal containing meat with a restaurant voucher

paper_authors: Mathur, M. B.; Peacock, J. R.; Robinson, T. N.; Gardner, C. D.
paper_title: Effectiveness of a theory-informed documentary to reduce consumption of meat and animal products: Three randomized controlled experiments
intervention_mechanism: info: health, info: environment, info: animal welfare, origin of animal product, norms: descriptive, goal pursuit: planning/pledge/reminder, goal pursuit: cooking skills
intervention_description: Participants watched a 20-min documentary entitled "Good For Us", which encourages plant-based diets that reduce consumption of all meats and animal products. At the end of the documentary participants saw a brief screen stating, “For practical tips on shifting to plant-based eating, see: www.eatingveg.org/how and www.eatingveg.org/what.” Each segment was followed by free-response questions about the content presented. After the documentary, participants were asked whether they would like to pledge to reduce their consumption, pledge to eliminate their consumption, or to make no pledge. For participants who had chosen to make at least one “reduce” or “eliminate” pledge, several items designed to improve general and specific goal-setting and self-monitoring were presented. One week after the baseline measurement, a customized reminder email was sent to pledge-making participants.
intervention_sample_description: participants from a Stanford University Prevention Research Center registry comprising previous participants of nutrition studies
control_description: Participants watched a 20-min TED talk by Brené Brown entitled “Listening to shame”, a generic motivational speech that encouraged listeners to embrace the experience of feeling vulnerable.
control_sample_description: participants from a Stanford University Prevention Research Center registry comprising previous participants of nutrition studies
outcome_label: reported meat consumption in the past week
outcome_description: Total weight of animal product consumption over the past week

paper_title: Nudging green food: The effects of a hedonic cue, menu position, a warm-glow cue, and a descriptive norm
intervention_description: Customers were presented with a digital menu (Swedish fast-food company MAX Burgers) featuring the message, "Many here choose green!", positioned next to the "Green" food category. This was accompanied by a minimalistic illustration of three human figures to reinforce the social norm
intervention_sample_description: data points represent individual sales at the Swedish fast food chain MAX Burgers
control_description: Customers were presented with original digital menu without any nudging adjustments, serving as the baseline for vegetarian sales.
control_sample_description: data points represent individual sales at the Swedish fast food chain MAX Burgers
outcome_description: Percentage of vegetarian sales

paper_authors: Caso, G.; Rizzo, G.; Migliore, G.; Vecchio, R.
paper_title: Loss framing effect on reducing excessive red and processed meat consumption: Evidence from Italy
intervention_description: Participants read that "The World Health Organization (WHO) classified red meat as a possible carcinogen, meaning it could cause cancer. Based on estimates from the Global Disease Burden Project, the WHO states that >34.000 cancer deaths per year worldwide are attributable to high intake of processed meat, while the total number of deaths attributable to a diet rich in red meat was 644.000."

control_description: Participants were not provided with any informational messaging regarding the consequences of excessive meat consumption.
control_sample_description: stratified Italian sample of meat eaters
outcome_description: Percentage of participants intending to consume less red and processed meat in the future

paper_title: Informational and emotional daily messages to reduce red and processed meat consumption
intervention_mechanism: info: environment, info: health, emotions: negative
intervention_description: Participants received daily persuasive messages that cited the health and environmental outcomes of red processed meat cosumption. The information was presented in a way that was meant to evoke anticipated regret (e.g., “If you eat an excessive amount of red/processed meat, you could feel regret for not protecting your health from cancer and the environment from the release of harmful greenhouse gases”).

control_description: Participants received daily persuasive messages describing the health and environmental consequences of eating sugar. control_sample_description: undergraduates of the Catholic University of Milan
outcome_description: Total number of processed red meat portions weighted by portion size

paper_authors: Dakin, B. C.; Ching, A. E.; Teperman, E.; Klebl, C.; Moshel, M.; Bastian, B.
paper_title: Prescribing vegetarian or flexitarian diets leads to sustained reduction in meat intake
intervention_description: Participants were briefed on the rationale for and requirements of the diet for the adherence peroiod, they were also given a handout including the following information: "One type of diet that some people endorse is a Climatarian diet significantly reducing or eliminating red meat from one's diet. The reasoning is as follows: Livestock agriculture contributes up to a quarter of global greenhouse gas emissions, with almost all of this coming from cows and sheep. In Australia, producing 1 kg of beef requires an average of 15,182 L of water. Furthermore, livestock agriculture drives 80% of deforestation worldwide, using up a third of the world s ice-free land. Adopting a Climatarian diet is a powerful way to reduce your impact on the climate. Shifting to a Climatarian diet will reduce your carbon footprint by approximately 1000 kg of CO2 every year, or the equivalent of driving from Perth to Brisbane."
intervention_sample_description: University of Melbourne participant pool
control_sample_description: University of Melbourne participant pool
outcome_description: Total servings of 10 common meat types over the past seven days

paper_authors: Dakin, B. C.; Ching, A. E.; Teperman, E.; Klebl, C.; Moshel, M.; Bastian, B.
paper_title: Prescribing vegetarian or flexitarian diets leads to sustained reduction in meat intake
intervention_mechanism: info: animal welfare, goal pursuit: efficacy/consequences/feedback
intervention_description: Participants were briefed on the rationale for and requirements of the diet for the adherence peroiod, they were also given a handout including the following information: "One type of diet that some people endorse is the ‘One step for the animals diet' eliminating chicken from our diets. The reasoning is as follows: Despite having the same capacity to suffer, farmed animals are denied the protection of animal cruelty laws that protect dogs and cats. This means that it is legal and widespread to cause immense suffering to animals raised for food.  If we can convince someone to stop eating birds, they would go from being responsible for the factory farming and slaughtering of more than two dozen land animals per year to fewer than one. Furthermore, while most cows and sheep raised in Australia spend most of their life grazing, the vast majority of chickens are raised in crowded factory farms."
intervention_sample_description: University of Melbourne participant pool
control_sample_description: University of Melbourne participant pool
outcome_description: Total servings of 10 common meat types over the past seven days

paper_authors: Dakin, B. C.; Ching, A. E.; Teperman, E.; Klebl, C.; Moshel, M.; Bastian, B.
paper_title: Prescribing vegetarian or flexitarian diets leads to sustained reduction in meat intake
intervention_mechanism: info: animal welfare, goal pursuit: efficacy/consequences/feedback
intervention_description: Participants were briefed on the rationale for and requirements of the diet for the adherence peroiod, they were also given a handout including the following information: "One type of diet that some people endorse is a Reducetarian diet reducing the amount of meat that we eat. The reasoning is as follows: Despite having the same capacity to suffer, farmed animals are denied the protection of animal cruelty laws that protect dogs and cats. This means that it is legal and widespread to cause immense suffering to animals raised for food. This includes painful surgical mutilations (without pain relief) and in most cases, intensive and unnatural overcrowding or confinement, and often a painful and traumatic slaughter. There are over 500 million animals confined like this in factory farms in Australia today. This includes cows, pigs, sheep and chickens. Adopting a reductionist diet is appealing because not everyone is willing to follow an all-or-nothing diet. It is also a powerful way to reduce animal cruelty. For example, halving your meat consumption will spare around 50 innocent lives every year. This includes about 13 land animals, and 37 fish and shellfish every year."
intervention_sample_description: University of Melbourne participant pool
control_sample_description: University of Melbourne participant pool
outcome_description: Total servings of 10 common meat types over the past seven days

paper_authors: Dakin, B. C.; Ching, A. E.; Teperman, E.; Klebl, C.; Moshel, M.; Bastian, B.
paper_title: Prescribing vegetarian or flexitarian diets leads to sustained reduction in meat intake
intervention_mechanism: info: animal welfare, goal pursuit: efficacy/consequences/feedback
intervention_description: Participants were briefed on the rationale for and requirements of the diet for the adherence peroiod, they were also given a handout including the following information:  "One type of diet that some people endorse is a Vegetarian diet eliminating all meat and fish from one's diet. The reasoning is as follows: Despite having the same capacity to suffer, farmed animals are denied the protection of animal cruelty laws that protect dogs and cats. This means that it is legal and widespread to cause immense suffering to animals raised for food. This includes painful surgical mutilations (without pain relief) and in most cases, intensive and unnatural overcrowding or confinement, and often a painful and traumatic slaughter. There are over 500 million animals confined like this in factory farms in Australia today. This includes cows, pigs, sheep and chickens. Adopting a vegetarian diet is the most powerful way to object to animal cruelty, and by doing so, you will spare approximately 100 animal lives (25 land animals and 75 fish and shellfish) every year."
intervention_sample_description: University of Melbourne participant pool
control_sample_description: University of Melbourne participant pool
outcome_description: Total servings of 10 common meat types over the past seven days

paper_authors: Branković, M.; Budžak, A.; Tulić, N.; Janković, J.
paper_title: Effects of experimentally induced self-affirmation on the openness to meat reduction and alternative protein sources
intervention_description: Participants were asked to create a ranked list of 10 values and briefly describe a situation or example that shows how the first-ranked value is reflected in their personal experience. Following values were presented: living in the present moment, being connected with friends and family, trust among people, loyalty and integrity, religious values, solidarity in society, sense of humor, contribution to society, democracy and creativity. Participants then read a vignette briefly introducing health-related risks of meat consumption (e.g., higher incidence of disease), benefits of decreased consumption, as well as alternative sources of protein, including lab-grown meats.
intervention_sample_description: Participants recruited via a passive snowball method on social media.
control_description: Participants were asked to create a ranked list of their favorite colors and explained how this color is personally appealing, e.g., to describe an object or context in which it was appealing. Participants then read a vignette briefly introducing health-related risks of meat consumption (e.g., higher incidence of disease), benefits of decreased consumption, as well as alternative sources of protein, including lab-grown meats.
control_sample_description: Participants recruited via a passive snowball method on social media.
outcome_description: Average openness to meat reduction measured with five items (e.g. their readiness to reduce meat consumption in the following 6 months)

intervention_description: "Chef’s recommendation" stickers were placed on the vegetarian sandwich bags. One vegetarian sandwich was displayed on a plate at eye-level with the content clearly visible. All vegetarian sandwiches were placed in first line-of-sight from the typical customer flow direction. The labels on vegetarian sandwiches were slightly more colourful and the bags were closed more aesthetically than meat sandwiches.

paper_title: Promoting plant-based eating in meat-centric meal contexts: A field study
intervention_mechanism: choice architecture: visibility/salience/ease, taste, info: environment
intervention_description: The menu offered a choice between three meal options per day including one with meat, one with fish, and one vegetarian, which changed daily. A 2-day workshop with the canteen kitchen staff on the relevance of promoting plant-based eating, basic principles of plant-based nutritional literacy, and practical demonstrations of how to improve the visual and sensory appeal of plant-based meals was conducted. Posters advertising the "Sustainability Week" initiative were placed in the cafeteria. The menu presented the vegetarian option as the "suggestion of the day" with an increased font size above the meat and fish options.

paper_authors: Winkelmair, A.; Jansen, P.
paper_title: The positive impact of mindfulness interventions on the explicit and implicit affective attitudes toward vegetarian foods
intervention_description: Participants completed a 12-week compassion and caring-based training which consisted of weekly sessions á 75 min and weekly homework assignments. Participants were introduced to a way to offer love to themselves, other people, or animals. Affect dyad situations were applied where partners sit face to face, and one partner contemplates a situation to a specific theme of the main topics. The other partner listens attentively without giving any verbal or non-verbal feedback. Each session included a talk about a specific topic. The main subjects of this course were breath, handling difficult emotions, the four Buddhist Brahmaviharas, prosociality, compassion, appreciative joy, equanimity, forgiveness, gratitude, and self-compassion.
intervention_sample_description: students from the University of Regensburg
control_sample_description: students from the University of Regensburg
outcome_description: Percentage of participants identifying as vegan or vegetarian

paper_authors: Winkelmair, A.; Jansen, P.
paper_title: The positive impact of mindfulness interventions on the explicit and implicit affective attitudes toward vegetarian foods
intervention_description: Participants completed a 12-week mindfulness-based stress reduction program that included various elements of meditation: body scan, mindful eating, emphasizing the breath, and sitting and walking meditation training. Every session contained a talk about specific topics such as different forms of meditation and problems that may arise with them. The focus of this mindfulness group was on the rather cognitive elements of mindfulness and its qualities of awareness. All sessions followed the same fixed sequence of quiet time, check-in, talk, meditation, exercise, homework, and check-out.
intervention_sample_description: students from the University of Regensburg
control_sample_description: students from the University of Regensburg
outcome_description: Percentage of participants identifying as vegan or vegetarian

paper_authors: Pohlmann, A.
paper_title: The taste of compassion: Influencing meat attitudes with interhuman and interspecies moral appeals
intervention_description: Participants were asked to evaluate photographic art prints that a restaurant is considering exhibiting in its dining areas: participants viewed a slideshow consisting of 15 pretested stock photographs portraying scenes of vulnerability, human connection, care-giving, and displays of emotional distress. The photos pictured female and male adults, as well as children and the elderly, and a range of ethnicities. Prior to this, in both the control and treatment conditions, with a visible timer counting down from 3 min, participants were asked to recall 10 behaviors where they acted “particularly masculine/manly” or “particularly feminine/womanly” (depending on their gender). Participants read that men and women can easily recall 12 behaviors. The task is intentionally difficult with the purpose of leading participants to infer that their performance is below purportedly acceptable standards for their respective gender, thus threatening gender identity.
intervention_sample_description: Residents in the United States.
control_description: Participants were asked to evaluate photographic art prints that a restaurant is considering exhibiting in its dining areas: participants viewed a slideshow consisting of geometric shapes. Prior to this, in both the control and treatment conditions, with a visible timer counting down from 3 min, participants were asked to recall 10 behaviors where they acted “particularly masculine/manly” or “particularly feminine/womanly” (depending on their gender). Participants read that men and women can easily recall 12 behaviors. The task is intentionally difficult with the purpose of leading participants to infer that their performance is below purportedly acceptable standards for their respective gender, thus threatening gender identity.
control_sample_description: Residents in the United States.
outcome_description: Percentage of participants selecting a meat entrée out of ten pairs of photographs of popular entrées that can be prepared with meat or in a vegetarian version

paper_authors: Pohlmann, A.

intervention_description: Installation of of visual manipulations under the guise of a photo exhibit at the restaurant. The photographic stimuli were chosen with the following in mind: the connection between human and animal needed to be emphasized by visual overlap between the subjects, suggesting a caring, devoted, and protecting attitude.
intervention_sample_description: Visitors of a restaurant near the university campus.
control_sample_description: Visitors of a restaurant near the university campus.
outcome_description: Number of meat menu item sales

In February 2019, scientists collaborated with The Lancet medical journal to establish this realistic diet that maintains both human and planetary health.” And on the bottom of the sign “Modeling your plate like this one builds a healthy diet and a sustainable world”.

intervention_description: All meat dishes were labeled as “High CO2” dishes, all fish dishes as “Medium CO2”, and all vegetarian as “Low CO2” dishes. The labels used a simple color-coded scheme in combination with words inside the labels to visualize the environmental impact of the dish to improve efficacy and they were placed on the menu board next to dish description where consumers ordered their food. In addition to labels, posters were placed in the cafeteria, explaining the labeling system and the climate impact of the different food categories.

paper_authors: Erhard, A.;  Boztuğ, Y.; Lemken, D.
paper_title: How do defaults and framing influence food choice? An intervention aimed at promoting plant-based choice in online menus
intervention_description: Participants viewed an online menu offered by a mock meal-kit delivery service and prior to viewing the menu, they read a definition of meal kits. Then they were instructed to imagine they were selecting a meal for themselves and each meal had the same cost. All menus presented the same dish, an orzo pasta salad, that could be ordered with either a plant-based or pork sausage on skewers. Each menu had a pre-selected sausage such that one type of sausage was in the electronic shopping cart upon viewing the menu. In the plant-based default condition, the plant-based sausage was selected.
intervention_sample_description: Prolific workers
control_description: Participants viewed an online menu offered by a mock meal-kit delivery service and prior to viewing the menu, they read a definition of meal kits. Then they were instructed to imagine they were selecting a meal for themselves and each meal had the same cost. All menus presented the same dish, an orzo pasta salad, that could be ordered with either a plant-based or pork sausage on skewers. Each menu had a pre-selected sausage such that one type of sausage was in the electronic shopping cart upon viewing the menu. The baseline condition, in which pork was pre-selected, was used to reflect the real-world status quo.
control_sample_description: Prolific workers
outcome_description: Percentage of times a meal with the plant-based sausage was selected in a mock meal-kit delivery service

paper_authors: Bianchi, F.; Stewart, C.; Astbury, N. M.; Cook, B.; Aveyard, P.; Jebb, S. A.
paper_title: Replacing meat with alternative plant-based products (RE-MAP): A randomized controlled trial of a multicomponent behavioral intervention to reduce meat consumption
paper_source: The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
intervention_mechanism: info: health, info: environment, goal pursuit: food provision, goal pursuit: cooking skills, priming, goal pursuit: efficacy/consequences/feedback
intervention_description: Participants were exposed to a four-week behavioral intervention targeting five psychosocial variables: attitudes, perceived behavioral control, and subjective social norms regarding a low-meat diet, alongside meat attachment and eating identities (e.g., "meat-eater" or "vegetarian"). The intervention utilized four behavioral change functions: environmental restructuring (provision of meat alternatives), training (provision of recipes), education (infographics on health and environmental benefits), and social modeling (vignettes detailing successful meat-reduction stories). Consequently, the intervention comprised four distinct components: (1) free household meat substitutes for four weeks; (2) educational leaflets; (3) recipes; and (4) success stories in the form of written vignettes.
intervention_sample_description: volunteers from Oxford, UK from adult-only households recruited from the community through advertisements
control_sample_description: volunteers from Oxford, UK from adult-only households recruited from the community through advertisements
outcome_description: Daily meat consumption

paper_source: The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
intervention_mechanism: info: health, info: environment, goal pursuit: food provision, goal pursuit: cooking skills, priming, goal pursuit: efficacy/consequences/feedback
intervention_description: Participants were exposed to a four-week behavioral intervention targeting five psychosocial variables: attitudes, perceived behavioral control, and subjective social norms regarding a low-meat diet, alongside meat attachment and eating identities (e.g., "meat-eater" or "vegetarian"). The intervention utilized four behavioral change functions: environmental restructuring (provision of meat alternatives), training (provision of recipes), education (infographics on health and environmental benefits), and social modeling (vignettes detailing successful meat-reduction stories). Consequently, the intervention comprised four distinct components: (1) free household meat substitutes for four weeks; (2) educational leaflets; (3) recipes; and (4) success stories in the form of written vignettes.
intervention_sample_description: volunteers from Oxford, UK from adult-only households recruited from the community through advertisements
control_sample_description: volunteers from Oxford, UK from adult-only households recruited from the community through advertisements
outcome_label: intentions to eat a low-meat diet
outcome_description: Intention to eat low-meat diet

paper_authors: Perino, G.; Schwirplies, C.
paper_title: Meaty arguments and fishy effects: Field experimental evidence on the impact of reasons to reduce meat consumption
paper_source: Journal of Environmental Economics and Management
intervention_description: First, participants read a ‘decoy’ newspaper quote regarding lunch breaks to reduce the salience of the quotes about meat consumption and prevent demand characteristics. Second, they read an excerpt from a real newspaper article advocating for a specific reason why reducing meat consumption is recommended (e.g., "Meat harms your health"). All quotes referred to "scientific evidence" or a "study" without naming a specific source. Each quote was followed by an explicit statement containing a dietary recommendation taken from the website of the German Nutrition Society.
intervention_sample_description: German panel participants
control_description: Participants read an excerpt from a real newspaper article regarding sugary drinks in school kiosks; this text served as a control and did not contain any reference to meat. As with the experimental conditions, each quote was followed by an explicit statement incorporating a dietary recommendation sourced from the German Nutrition Society.
control_sample_description: German panel participants
outcome_description: Intention to reduce meat consumption by a little or a lot in the coming weeks

paper_authors: Çoker, E. N.; Pechey, R.; Frie, K.; Jebb, S. A.; Stewart, C.; Higgs, S.; Cook, B.
paper_title: A dynamic social norm messaging intervention to reduce meat consumption: A randomized cross-over trial in retail store restaurants
intervention_description: Participants were exposed to social norm message for two consecutive weeks, preceded by two weeks of no-message control. The message “More and more [retail store name] customers are choosing our veggie options” appeared in a green circle on the top right corner of the image displayed on the digital boards, covering 1/8th of the screen, and was animated with a small stretching and wobbling effect to draw customers’ attention further. The message was displayed alongside a picture of the vegetarian breakfast option during the breakfast service hours, and a vegetarian main meal during the remaining trading hours.
intervention_sample_description: patrons of in-store restaurants of a retail chain in the UK
control_sample_description: patrons of in-store restaurants of a retail chain in the UK
outcome_description: Percentage of main meals that were plant-based

paper_authors: Zinn, A. K.; Zhu, O. Y.; Dolnicar, S.
paper_title: Increasing meat-free meal selections: The role of social identity salience and identity-related meal names
intervention_description: Participants completed an identity manipulation: Please think of yourself as an individual (personal identity). Please write at least 4–5 sentences (25 words) about the following topic: What are things that you personally do ... often? ... rarely? ... well? ... badly?. While asking about both negative and positive aspects, this manipulation aimed to activate a specific identity without emotional confounds. Meal name manipulation: the vegetarian burgers were labeled as a sustainable halloumi burger and a sustainable falafel burger.
intervention_sample_description: Australian and British Prolific workers
control_description: Participants completed an identity manipulation: Please think of yourself as an individual (personal identity). Please write at least 4–5 sentences (25 words) about the following topic: What are things that you personally do ... often? ... rarely? ... well? ... badly?. control_sample_description: Australian and British Prolific workers
outcome_description: Percentage of participants selecting a vegetarian burger

paper_authors: Garnett, E. E.; Balmford, A; Marteau, T. M.; Pilling, M. A.; Sandbrook, C.
paper_title: Price of change: Does a small alteration to the price of meat and vegetarian options affect their sales?
intervention_description: The college served two meat options, one vegetarian, one fish and one vegan option at all mealtimes, except on Friday lunchtimes where one meat option was replaced with an additional fish option and Saturday lunchtimes where the vegan option was not included. In the final five weeks of the term, the college cafeteria decreased the price of the vegetarian option by 20 pence (from £2.05 to £1.85) and increased the price of meat options by 20 pence (from £2.52 to £2.72).
intervention_sample_description: data points represent meals served at a university cafeteria
control_sample_description: data points represent meals served at a university cafeteria
outcome_description: Percentage of main meals that were vegetarian

paper_title: When are “Dish of the Day” nudges most effective to increase vegetable selection?
intervention_description: The choices were presented orally to consumers by the experimenter, who said “Today as a main dish, you have a choice between the options shown on the menu board”. The dish options were shown on a clearly visible menu board, posted at the point of choice. In conditions with a dish of the day, the experimenter (impersonating a waitress/waiter) said “today our Dish of the day is ___. Other alternatives are available on the menu board” and showed the menu board, which featured the dish of the day and the other alternatives. The vegetarian option (veggie burger) was labeled as the dish of the day.
intervention_sample_description: French customers of a "living lab" restaurant
control_sample_description: French customers of a "living lab" restaurant
outcome_description: Percentage of customers choosing the vegetarian dish advertised as the dish of the day

paper_authors: Karakaş, N., Jaeger, B.
paper_title: Changes in attitudes toward meat consumption after chatting with a large language model
intervention_mechanism: other, info: health, info: environment, info: animal welfare
intervention_description: Participants had an open-ended chat about meat consumption that lasted 5-10 minutes with ChatGPT-4o. The same initial prompt was entered for all participants: “I want to have a conversation about meat consumption. I currently eat meat. Are there any reasons why I should limit my meat consumption or stop eating meat entirely?”. Participants were given a list of questions they could ask ChatGPT, but were also told that they could decide where to steer the conversation. The provided questions focused on the environment (e.g., “How does meat consumption impact the environment?”), personal health (e.g., “Is it necessary to eat meat to be healthy?”), and animal welfare (e.g., “What percentage of meat in Dutch supermarkets comes from factory farms?”), and also included more generic prompts (e.g., “It would be quite difficult to reduce my meat consumption by a lot. How can I make this easier?”).
intervention_sample_description: students recruited in the library of a Dutch university
control_sample_description: students recruited in the library of a Dutch university
outcome_description: Average score on the Meat Commitment Scale

paper_authors: Altema-Johnson, D.; Hernandez, R.; Nowara, A.; Ramsing, B.; Wingood, G.
paper_title: Evaluation of the meatless monday campaign at NewYork- Presbyterian
intervention_description: The intervention intended to educate employees on how small adjustments in meat consumption can reduce an individual’s environmental footprint as well as the risk of adverse health outcomes associated with high-meat diets. Participants joined an 18-month Meatless Monday campaign in which cafeterias promoted a discounted and dietitian approved vegetarian dish alongside the available meat options. Weekly posters delivered evidence-based health and environmental messages regarding the benefits of meat reduction.
intervention_sample_description: The intervention is implemented in four facilities of New York Presbyterian dining cafeterias and participants were NewYork Presbyterian (NYP) employees
control_sample_description: The intervention is implemented in four facilities of New York Presbyterian dining cafeterias and participants were NewYork Presbyterian (NYP) employees
outcome_label: percentage of purchasing a meatless entrée/meal
outcome_description: Number of participants purchasing a meatless entrée/meal ordered in NYPcafeteria.

outcome_label: cutting back the amount of consumed meat
outcome_description: Participants were asked a multiple-choice question regarding whether they try to cut back on the amount of meat they eat. They could select from the following options: (a) Yes, I am actively trying to cut back; (b) No, but I have cut back on meat in the past; (c) No, but I am considering cutting back; (d) No, I am not considering cutting back (skip to question 6B); (e) I do not eat meat; (f) I'm not sure. The outcome represents the percentage of participants trying to cut back on their meat consumption.

paper_authors: Dannenberg, A.; Weingärtner, E.
paper_title: The effects of observability and an information nudge on food choice
intervention_description: Participants were eligible to win a transferable voucher for two sandwiches (meat, vegetarian, or vegan options), redeemable at a local establishment. Participants read the following information on meat consumption reduction: "Reducing one's consumption of animal products can have significant environmental benefits. For example, food-related greenhouse gas emissions can on average be lowered by about a quarter (24%) through following a vegetarian diet and by about half (53%) through following a vegan diet." before privately announced their choice of voucher.
intervention_sample_description: Bachelor and Master students who were having an online lecture at a university in Germany
control_description: Participants were eligible to win a transferable voucher for two sandwiches (meat, vegetarian, or vegan options), redeemable at a local establishment. No information on meat consumption were given, participants read the following text just before they made their decision on which sandwich they wanted (meat, vegetarian or vegan): "The winners will be publicly announced directly in the breakout room. Their choice of voucher will not be revealed to other participants. Please indicate which type of voucher you would like to receive if you win."
control_sample_description: Bachelor and Master students who were having an online lecture at a university in Germany
outcome_description: Percentage of participants choosing the voucher for a meat sandwich

control_sample_description: Bachelor and Master students who were having an online lecture at a university in Germany
outcome_description: Percentage of participants choosing the voucher for a meat sandwich

paper_authors: Dannenberg, A.; Weingärtner, E.
paper_title: The effects of observability and an information nudge on food choice
intervention_description: Participants were eligible to win a transferable voucher for two sandwiches (meat, vegetarian, or vegan options), redeemable at a local establishment. Participants read the following text just before they made their decision on which sandwich they wanted: "The winners will be publicly announced directly in the breakout room and asked for their choice of voucher so that we can pass on the selection to the restaurant. Please indicate which type of voucher you would like to receive if you win." Participants read the following information on meat consumption reduction before their choice of voucher was publicly announced: "Reducing one's consumption of animal products can have significant environmental benefits. For example, food-related greenhouse gas emissions can on average be lowered by about a quarter (24%) through following a vegetarian diet and by about half (53%) through following a vegan diet."
intervention_sample_description: Bachelor and Master students who were having an online lecture at a university in Germany
control_description: Participants were eligible to win a transferable voucher for two sandwiches (meat, vegetarian, or vegan options), redeemable at a local establishment. No information on meat consumption were given, participants read the following text just before they made their decision on which sandwich they wanted (meat, vegetarian or vegan): "The winners will be publicly announced directly in the breakout room. Their choice of voucher will not be revealed to other participants. Please indicate which type of voucher you would like to receive if you win."
control_sample_description: Bachelor and Master students who were having an online lecture at a university in Germany
outcome_description: Percentage of participants choosing the voucher for a meat sandwich

paper_authors: Weikertova, S.; Urban, J.
paper_title: Dynamic norms do not promote vegetarian food preferences: Two experiments and a meta-Analysis
intervention_description: Participants received dynamic norm treatment by reading three texts, each one paragraph long; the first and the third text contain information about dynamic social norms regarding the increasing share of meatless meals and decreasing share of meat-based meals in the Czech Republic and among students respectively.
intervention_sample_description: Participants were Charles University Students obtained through snowball sampling via email lists and facebook posts.
control_description: Participants received unrelated trends (trends in eating at home) information.
control_sample_description: Participants were Charles University Students obtained through snowball sampling via email lists and facebook posts.
outcome_description: Participants' choice of meatless meals in a choice experiment task including one meat-based and one meatless meal. These meals adapted from university canteens, not labeled,as vegetarian or not, and they had a fixed price.

paper_authors: Schwantes, C.
paper_title: The meat of the matter: Using social marketing to influence red meat purchase behaviour
intervention_mechanism: info: health, info: environment, identity/reputation, goal pursuit: planning/pledge/reminder
intervention_description: A public commitment campaign was tested at an intervention store, to determine whether it could reduce red meat purchase frequency. Customers entering the intervention store were asked the following question: “To consider a healthy and sustainable lifestyle, would you be interested in reducing your red meat purchases by one meal per week?” If customers agreed, they were asked to demonstrate their commitment by having their name listed on the public-facing project website, www.meatofthematerstudy.squarespace.com. They were also given a small business card with the project website to see their name and learn more about the study.
intervention_sample_description: shoppers at a grocery store
control_sample_description: shoppers at a grocery store
outcome_description: Amount of red meat purchased at the grocery store on average

paper_authors: Rieforth, A.; Theis, H.
paper_title: Nudging to reduce meat consumption: Can indulgent or healthy descriptors promote vegetarian food choices?
intervention_description: Following a fictional background story regarding a new food box delivery start-up, participants were presented with descriptors and pictures of three food boxes (and later sandwiches and salads) from which they were asked to choose one. Vegetarian food box options described with indulgent words-phrases such as "soul comforting", "feel good'', ''fresh and crispy''. Meat food box options had basic descriptions in all conditions.
intervention_sample_description: Participants, the majority of whom resided in Europe (88.5%), were recruited through snowball sampling via contacts and social networks.
control_description: Following a fictional background story regarding a new food box delivery start-up, participants were presented with descriptors and pictures of three food boxes (and later sandwiches and salads) from which they were asked to choose one. Vegetarian food box options included basic descriptions in this condition, and served as a baseline. Meat food box options had basic descriptions in all conditions.
control_sample_description: Participants, the majority of whom resided in Europe (88.5%), were recruited through snowball sampling via contacts and social networks.
outcome_description: The recording of the food option that each participant selected in their assigned survey

paper_authors: Mattson, S.
paper_title: Analyzing the effectiveness of a meatless monday intervention on meat consumption and associated pro-environmental spillover behavior throughout the Week
intervention_mechanism: info: environment, norms: descriptive, norms: dynamic, authority/role models, goal pursuit: efficacy/consequences/feedback
intervention_description: In addition to the baseline survey and upcoming week's food diary information, participants received an intervention in the form of a digital flyer encouraging participation in Meatless Monday, and prime their environmental identity, increase awareness of the environmental impacts of meat production, activate social norms, and decrease perceived behavioral difficulty. Participants were also asked about their relationship to the Meatless Monday movement, including prior awareness of the movement, past participation rates, and future participation intentions.
intervention_sample_description: Students were recruited from four undergraduate courses offered by School   of   Environment   and   Natural   Resources   (SENR)   at  The   Ohio   State   University
control_description: Participants filled the baseline survey, and received an email with instructions stating that they would be emailed individual links to online food diaries each morning of the upcoming week (Monday through Sunday, seven separate emails) and that they would have 48 hours to complete

outcome_description: Reported Total Percentages of Meat-Containing Meals from Monday through Sunday Following Intervention

paper_authors: Attwood, S.; Chesworth, S. J.; Parkin, B. L.
paper_title: Menu engineering to encourage sustainable food choices when dining out: An online trial of priced-based decoys
intervention_description: Participants were provided with the following instructions prior to seeing each of the four menus “Please consider the menu on the following page. Imagine you are in a restaurant; please select which dish you would be most likely to order”. To make their choice between the three dishes, participants clicked on their desired option, after which they were directed to the subsequent menu. They saw four menus that included a meat option, a vegetarian option, and a decoy vegetarian option (it is 2 GBP more expensive than the other two items).
intervention_sample_description: participants were recruited from a participant pool at the University of Westminister
control_description: Participants were provided with the following instructions prior to seeing each of the four menus “Please consider the menu on the following page. Imagine you are in a restaurant; please select which dish you would be most likely to order”. To make their choice between the three dishes, participants clicked on their desired option, after which they were directed to the subsequent menu. For control condition, they saw four menus that included a meat option, a vegetarian option, and another vegetarian option, all of the same price.
control_sample_description: participants were recruited from a participant pool at the University of Westminister
outcome_description: Number of participants choosing the meat option from the menu

paper_authors: Attwood, S.; Chesworth, S. J.; Parkin, B. L.
paper_title: Menu engineering to encourage sustainable food choices when dining out: An online trial of priced-based decoys
intervention_description: Participants were asked to choose between three dishes– a ‘competitor’ meat option, a ‘target’ vegetarian option and a ‘decoy’ vegetarian option. Participants in the intervention group were shown the decoy vegetarian option listed at a price four pounds more expensive than other options on the menu (with the exception of the soup, which is a cheaper dish, and hence a 30% price rise was applied in line with the magnitude of the price differential in the other conditions).
intervention_sample_description: UK Prolific Academic workers
control_description: Participants were asked to choose between three dishes– a ‘competitor’ meat option, a ‘target’ vegetarian option and a ‘decoy’ vegetarian option. Participants in the control group were exposed to all three options at the base price.
control_sample_description: UK Prolific Academic workers
outcome_description: Number of participants choosing the meat option from the menu
