paper_authors: Lohmann, P. M.; Gsottbauerb, E.; Farringtone, J.; Human, S.; Reisch, L. A.
paper_title: Choice architecture promotes sustainable choices in online food-delivery apps
intervention_description: Participants were presented with a simulated delivery platform, with an additional tax imposed on items containing meat. The platform included nine restaurants that were based on real-world equivalents and offered a variety of popular cuisines. Each restaurant’s menu included a selection of starters, mains, desserts, and drinks (if applicable), to make the choice environment as realistic as possible. In total, participants could select from 164 unique food items for which we calculated the carbon footprint and calorie content. The menu page of a respective item displayed a red “T” icon and the text “Includes £X.XX of meat tax.” In the selection pop-up and in the checkout basket, an extra row identified the price added by the tax as “+£X.XX meat tax.” Additional rows displayed a small information icon, providing further details about the tax’s purpose and impact (“An additional meat tax has been added to this item to reflect its climate impact”) if hovered over with the mouse or touched on a mobile device.
intervention_sample_description: British individuals who actively used delivery apps and lived in ubran areas
control_description: Participants were presented with a simulated delivery platform in its regular “business as usual” design. They saw the same pages without carbon footprint or tax information, and both restaurant and menu pages were presented randomly.
control_sample_description: British individuals who actively used delivery apps and lived in ubran areas
outcome_description: Proportion of meat or fish mains chosen

paper_authors: Lohmann, P. M.; Gsottbauerb, E.; Farringtone, J.; Human, S.; Reisch, L. A.
paper_title: Choice architecture promotes sustainable choices in online food-delivery apps
intervention_description: Participants were presented with a simulated delivery platform, with additional information in the form of carbon-footprint labels included on the menu page of every food item and selection pop-up. The platform included nine restaurants that were based on real-world equivalents and offered a variety of popular cuisines. Each restaurant’s menu included a selection of starters, mains, desserts, and drinks (if applicable), to make the choice environment as realistic as possible. In total, participants could select from 164 unique food items for which we calculated the carbon footprint and calorie contentMeal items are rated based on their carbon footprint per kilogram using categories from A (very low) to E (very high), following the Global Carbon Budget for Food (2019 EAT-Lancet Commission).
intervention_sample_description: British individuals who actively used delivery apps and lived in ubran areas
control_description: Participants were presented with a simulated delivery platform in its regular “business as usual” design. They saw the same pages without carbon footprint or tax information, and both restaurant and menu pages were presented randomly.
control_sample_description: British individuals who actively used delivery apps and lived in ubran areas
outcome_description: Proportion of meat or fish mains chosen

paper_authors: Lohmann, P. M.; Gsottbauerb, E.; Farringtone, J.; Human, S.; Reisch, L. A.
paper_title: Choice architecture promotes sustainable choices in online food-delivery apps
intervention_description: The simulated delivery platform design that participants were presented with was altered to emphasize low-carbon alternatives. The platform included nine restaurants that were based on real-world equivalents and offered a variety of popular cuisines. Each restaurant’s menu included a selection of starters, mains, desserts, and drinks (if applicable), to make the choice environment as realistic as possible. In total, participants could select from 164 unique food items for which we calculated the carbon footprint and calorie content. Both the restaurant and menu pages were re-ordered, presenting options in decreasing order of sustainability, i.e., the lowest-carbon restaurants and meal items (within each menu category) were presented first.
intervention_sample_description: British individuals who actively used delivery apps and lived in ubran areas
control_description: Participants were presented with a simulated delivery platform in its regular “business as usual” design. They saw the same pages without carbon footprint or tax information, and both restaurant and menu pages were presented randomly.
control_sample_description: British individuals who actively used delivery apps and lived in ubran areas
outcome_description: Proportion of meat or fish mains chosen

paper_title: Impact of pictorial warning labels on meat meal selection: A randomised experimental study with UK meat consumers
intervention_description: Participants saw a meat option with a pictorial and textual warning label that eating meat contributes to climate change. Textual messages also contained references to the scientific sources where the warning messages pertaining to climate consequences of meat consumption came from. In all instances the scientific sources were acclaimed.
intervention_sample_description: UK Prolific workers with sampling quotas set for age and gender
control_description: Participants only saw meat option without any warning labels were shown.
control_sample_description: UK Prolific workers with sampling quotas set for age and gender
outcome_description: Willingness and intention to reduce meat consumption in the near future

paper_authors: Hughes, J. P.; Weick, M.; Vasiljevic, M.
paper_title: Impact of pictorial warning labels on meat meal selection: A randomised experimental study with UK meat consumers
intervention_description: Participants saw a meat option with a pictorial and textual warning label that eating meat contributes to pandemics. Textual messages also contained references to the scientific sources where the warning messages pertaining to pandemic consequences of meat consumption came from. In all instances the scientific sources were acclaimed.
intervention_sample_description: UK Prolific workers with sampling quotas set for age and gender
control_description: Participants only saw meat option without any warning labels were shown.
control_sample_description: UK Prolific workers with sampling quotas set for age and gender
outcome_description: Willingness and intention to reduce meat consumption in the near future

paper_authors: Katare, B.; Yim, H.; Byrne, A.; Wang, H. H.; Wetzstein, N.
paper_title: Consumer willingness to pay for environmentally sustainable meat and a plant-based meat substitute
intervention_description: All the participants were endowed with $10 and a 6-oz. package of farm-raised sliced deli beef. In addition, they were given information about the current market (base) price of the product. Treatment group was presented with information regarding the health and environmental benefits of consuming a plant-based meat substitute versus beef prior to bidding
intervention_sample_description: US Large scale university students
control_description: All the participants were endowed with $10 and a 6-oz. package of farm-raised sliced deli beef. In addition, they were given information about the current market (base) price of the product. Control group were not provided any information prior to their bidding.
control_sample_description: US Large scale university students
outcome_description: The amount of money (premium) a participant is willing to pay to replace their endowed 6-oz. sliced deli beef with 6 oz. of plant-based meat substitute.

paper_authors: Segovia, M. S.; Yu, N. Y.; Van Loo, E. J.
paper_title: The effect of information nudges on online purchases of meat alternatives
intervention_description: Participants were presented with a shopping list consisting of meat and plant-based meat alternatives during an online grocery shopping task. Next to the meat and plant-based alternatives, there was a health message emphasizing the health benefits of consuming less meat: “To reduce your risk of diabetes by 40%, eat one less serving of meat every day"
intervention_sample_description: respondents recruited on campus of an American university and via the university email
control_description: Participants were presented with a shopping list consisting of meat and plant-based meat alternatives during an online grocery shopping task.
control_sample_description: respondents recruited on campus of an American university and via the university email
outcome_description: Proportion of plant-based meat purchases

paper_authors: Segovia, M. S.; Yu, N. Y.; Van Loo, E. J.
paper_title: The effect of information nudges on online purchases of meat alternatives
intervention_description: Participants were presented with a shopping list consisting of meat and plant-based meat alternatives during an online grocery shopping task. Next to the meat and plant-based alternatives, there was a environment message emphasizing the environmental benefits of lower meat intake: “To reduce your environmental impact by 40%, eat one less serving of meat every day"
intervention_sample_description: respondents recruited on campus of an American university and via the university email
control_description: Participants were presented with a shopping list consisting of meat and plant-based meat alternatives during an online grocery shopping task.
control_sample_description: respondents recruited on campus of an American university and via the university email
outcome_description: Proportion of plant-based meat purchases

intervention_description: Participants were presented with a shopping list consisting of meat and plant-based meat alternatives during an online grocery shopping task. intervention_sample_description: respondents recruited on campus of an American university and via the university email
control_description: Participants were presented with a shopping list consisting of meat and plant-based meat alternatives during an online grocery shopping task.

paper_authors: De-loyde, K.; Pilling, M. A.; Thornton, A.; Spencer, G.; Maynard, O. M.
paper_title: Promoting sustainable diets using eco-labelling and social nudges: A randomised online experiment
intervention_description: Participants were presented with a menu that had three options including beef burrito, a chicken burrito and a vegetarian burrito, alongside a traffic light system, with a scale of 1–5, which was circled at the appropriate sustainability level for that burrito: beef burrito – unsustainable, chicken burrito – neither sustainable nor unsustainable and vegetarian burrito – sustainable
intervention_sample_description: UK Prolific workers
control_description: Participants were presented with a menu that had three options including beef burrito, a chicken burrito and a vegetarian burrito.
control_sample_description: UK Prolific workers
outcome_description: Percentage of participants choosing the beef burrito option

paper_authors: De-loyde, K.; Pilling, M. A.; Thornton, A.; Spencer, G.; Maynard, O. M.
paper_title: Promoting sustainable diets using eco-labelling and social nudges: A randomised online experiment
intervention_description: Participants were presented with a menu that had three options including beef burrito, a chicken burrito and a vegetarian burrito, where the vegetarian burrito was presented alongside a popularity label consisting of a gold star next to the words ‘Most Popular’
intervention_sample_description: UK Prolific workers
control_description: Participants were presented with a menu that had three options including beef burrito, a chicken burrito and a vegetarian burrito.
control_sample_description: UK Prolific workers
outcome_description: Percentage of participants choosing the beef burrito option

paper_authors: Choueiki, Z.; Geuens, M.; Vermeir, R.
paper_title: Animals like us: Leveraging the negativity bias in anthropomorphism to reduce beef consumption
intervention_description: Participants were presented with beef patties that had the intelligence anthropomorphic sticker "Cows can form associations and learn to solve simple problems like we do! Please reduce your meat consumption!"
intervention_sample_description: Prolific worker
control_description: After providing their consent, participants were told that they will be seeing a burger product which they needed to evaluate. They were informed that there are no right or wrong answers but that only their opinions matter. Each respondent was randomly assigned to one of five conditions and saw a package of a product with an on-pack sticker (or no sticker). For this control condition, participants were presented with beef patties that didn't have a sticker on them.
control_sample_description: Prolific worker
outcome_description: Intention to purchase the beef patty

paper_authors: Carfora, V.; Zeiske, N.; van der Werff, E.; Steg, L.; Catellani, P.
paper_title: Adding dynamic norm to environmental information in messages Promoting the reduction of meat consumption
intervention_description: Participants received persuasive messages from a private chatbot every other day, focused on the environmental consequences of producing and eating meat (e.g. “If the livestock industry is reduced, the widespread deforestation, caused by the need for pastures, will be avoided”)
intervention_sample_description: Italian young adults between 18 and 30 years
control_sample_description: Italian young adults between 18 and 30 years
outcome_description: Attitude towards reduced meat consumption

paper_authors: Carfora, V.; Zeiske, N.; van der Werff, E.; Steg, L.; Catellani, P.
paper_title: Adding dynamic norm to environmental information in messages Promoting the reduction of meat consumption
intervention_description: Participants received persuasive messages from a private chatbot every other day, focused on the environmental consequences of producing and eating meat, with the addition of information on the dynamic norm (e.g. “If the livestock industry is reduced, the widespread deforestation, caused by the need for pastures, will be avoided. To avoid deforestation, more and more people are reducing their consumption of meat”)
intervention_sample_description: Italian young adults between 18 and 30 years
control_sample_description: Italian young adults between 18 and 30 years
outcome_description: Attitude towards reduced meat consumption

paper_authors: dos Santos, Q.; Perez-Cueto, F.J.A.; Rodrigues, V.M.; Appleton, K.; Giboreau, A.; Saulais, L.; Monteleone, E.
paper_title: Impact of a nudging intervention and factors associated with vegetable dish choice among European adolescents
intervention_description: Participants were asked to choose between three meals, based, respectively, on Meat balls, Veggie balls and Fish cakes. All three dishes were labelled with their names, but the Veggie balls were presented as the “dish of the day”. The vegetable-based dish was both labelled as “dish of the day”, and orally informed by foodservice employees as being the “dish of the day” in all countries with the following phrase: “Welcome! Today we have Veggie balls as dish of the day” (verbal prompt).
intervention_sample_description: European adolescents from Denmark, France, Italy and the UK
control_description: Participants were asked to choose between three identical dishes to the intervention condition (Meat balls, Veggie balls and Fish cakes) were just labelled with their names and no “dish of the day” was offered.
control_sample_description: European adolescents from Denmark, France, Italy and the UK
outcome_description: Proportion of meat balls dish chosen

paper_authors: Van Loo, E. J.; Caputo, V.; Lusk, J. L.
paper_title: Consumer preferences for farm-raised meat, lab-grown meat, and plant-based meat alternatives: Does information or brand matter?
intervention_description: Participants were randomly assigned to one of the four blocks and answered nine choice questions, the order of which was randomized. Each choice question included a non-purchase (opt-out) alternative and four meat products or meat replacers offered at different prices. Participants were shown environmental information that originated from the companies selling these products (i.e. reduction in water use, land use, energy use and greenhouse gas emissions associated to each of the three beef alternatives compared to conventional beef)
intervention_sample_description: U.S. food shoppers were recruited by a market research agency, considering quotas for regions, gender, and race and ethnicity
control_description: Participants were randomly assigned to one of the four blocks and answered nine choice questions, the order of which was randomized. Each choice question included a non-purchase (opt-out) alternative and four meat products or meat replacers offered at different prices. Participants were asked to choose between lab-grown beef, plant-based meat animal-like heme protein, plant-based meat using pea protein, and farm-raised beef, with no additional information given about the products.
control_sample_description: U.S. food shoppers were recruited by a market research agency, considering quotas for regions, gender, and race and ethnicity
outcome_description: Percentage of beef item chosen

paper_authors: Marette, S.; Roosen, J.; Blanchemanche, S.
paper_title: Health information and substitution between fish: Lessons from laboratory and field experiments
intervention_description: Participants received a message based on advisories provided by health agencies, revealing health information about the risks and benefits of fish consumption; this was read out loud to them and they received a brochure with the same information.
intervention_sample_description: households with (i) at least one woman between 25 and 35 years of age, (ii) at least one child under 15 and (iii) who consume fish at least twice a week
control_description: Participants in the control group received no information.
control_sample_description: households with (i) at least one woman between 25 and 35 years of age, (ii) at least one child under 15 and (iii) who consume fish at least twice a week
outcome_label: all fish and sea foods consumption
outcome_description: Average values of the weekly serving frequency of all fish and sea foods for all women participating

paper_authors: Zhou, X.; Perez-Cueto, F. J. A.; dos Santos, Q.; Bredie, W. L.P.;  Brugarolas Mollá-Bauza, M. M.; Rodrigues, V. M.; Buch-Andersen, T.; Appleton, K. M.; Hemingway, A.; Giboreau, A.; Saulais, L.; Monteleone, E.; Dinnella, C.; Hartwell, H.
paper_title: Promotion of novel plant-based dishes among older consumers using the ‘dish of the day’ as a nudging strategy in 4 EU countries
intervention_description: Participants were shown a menu with meat balls, fish cakes, and a veggie balls dish which was highlighted as the dish of the day
intervention_sample_description: Elderly participants (65+) recruited through phone calls to the senior activity centres and through emails to the University of Copenhagen’s consumer panel
control_description: Participants were shown a menu with meat balls, fish cakes, and a veggie balls dish (unlike in the intervention condition, this was not highlighted as the dish of the day).
control_sample_description: Elderly participants (65+) recruited through phone calls to the senior activity centres and through emails to the University of Copenhagen’s consumer panel
outcome_description: Number of participants choosing the veggie balls option from the menu

paper_authors: Zhou, X.; Perez-Cueto, F. J. A.; dos Santos, Q.; Bredie, W. L.P.;  Brugarolas Mollá-Bauza, M. M.; Rodrigues, V. M.; Buch-Andersen, T.; Appleton, K. M.; Hemingway, A.; Giboreau, A.; Saulais, L.; Monteleone, E.; Dinnella, C.; Hartwell, H.
paper_title: Promotion of novel plant-based dishes among older consumers using the ‘dish of the day’ as a nudging strategy in 4 EU countries
intervention_description: Participants were shown a menu with meat balls, fish cakes, and a veggie balls dish which was highlighted as the dish of the day
intervention_sample_description: Elderly participants (65) recruited by emails to the internal consumer database of Institute Paul Bocuse, as well as online advertisements
control_description: Participants were shown a menu with meat balls, fish cakes, and a veggie balls dish (unlike in the intervention condition, this was not highlighted as the dish of the day).

paper_authors: Weingarten, N.; Meraner, M.; Bach, L.; Hartmann, M.
paper_title: Can information influence meat consumption behaviour? An experimental field study in the university canteen
intervention_description: Participants received information by a text composed of four paragraph. The first paragraph was identical in the health and environmental condition and informed about high meat consumption rates in Germany. The other paragraphs described negative impacts of meat consumption on health (e.g. increased risk of non-communicable diseases)
intervention_sample_description: students from a university in Germany who regularly visited the university canteen
control_description: Participants received information described the company that is responsible for the university canteen and included a recommendation to visit their website to get information about ongoing activities.
control_sample_description: students from a university in Germany who regularly visited the university canteen
outcome_description: All meat meal purchases two weeks after study participation (10 working days); Meat consumption behaviour was measured based on purchases of participants in the university canteen

paper_authors: Kranzbühler, A. M.; Schifferstein, H. N.J.
paper_title: The effect of meat-shaming on meat eaters' emotions and intentions to adapt behavior
intervention_description: Participants saw a supermarket package for chicken breast meat that had a sticker with a confronting image of chickens in a battery cage with the text ‘Eating meat makes animals suffer'. After providing written consent, participants were asked to imagine that they were shopping at their local supermarket, looking for some chicken to make for dinner.

control_description: Participants saw a supermarket package for chicken breast meat with no sticker. After providing written consent, participants were asked to imagine that they were shopping at their local supermarket, looking for some chicken to make for dinner.
control_sample_description: US MTurk participants
outcome_description: Intentions to buy the meat meal depicted in the picture

paper_authors: Pauer, S.; Rutjens, B. T.; Ruby, M. B.; Perino, G.; van Harreveld, F.
paper_title: Meating conflict: Toward a model of ambivalence-motivated reduction of meat consumption
intervention_description: The intervention focused on ambivalance manipulation via an ecologically valid procedure that has frequently been used by having participants introspect on their preexisting incongruent evaluations or personal experiences, and thus increase the simultaneous accessibility of attitudinal inconsistency. For this intervention, participants were asked to describe two personally held reactions in favor of and two reactions opposed to meat consumption.
intervention_sample_description: MTurk Participants mainly from North America (90%), and more than half of them had university degrees.
control_description: Participants were asked to reflect on their inconsistent evaluations about vaccination, they were asked to describe two personally held reactions in favor of and two reactions opposed to vaccination.
control_sample_description: MTurk Participants mainly from North America (90%), and more than half of them had university degrees.
outcome_description: Participants' intention to reduce meat consumption, in this case, it was measured with a 2 item scale - the future consumption was mentioned to be next week

paper_authors: Pauer, S.; Rutjens, B. T.; Ruby, M. B.; Perino, G.; van Harreveld, F.
paper_title: Meating conflict: Toward a model of ambivalence-motivated reduction of meat consumption
intervention_description: The intervention focused on ambivalance manipulation via an ecologically valid procedure that has frequently been used by having participants introspect on their preexisting incongruent evaluations or personal experiences, and thus increase the simultaneous accessibility of attitudinal inconsistency. For this intervention, participants were asked to describe two personally held reactions in favor of and two reactions opposed to meat consumption.
intervention_sample_description: Prolific Participants from England with 60.7% university degrees
control_description: Participants were asked to describe two personally held reactions in favor of and two reactions opposed to going to cinema in order to make them reflect on their inconsistent evaluations about going to the cinema.
control_sample_description: Prolific Participants from England with 60.7% university degrees
outcome_description: Participants' intention to reduce meat consumption, in this case, it was measured with a 2 item scale - the future consumption was mentioned to be next week

paper_authors: Lim, T. J.; Okine, R. N.; Kershaw, J. C.
paper_title: Health- or environment-focused text messages as a potential strategy to increase plant-based eating among young adults: An exploratory study.
intervention_mechanism: info: health, norms: dynamic, goal pursuit: cooking skills, goal pursuit: planning/pledge/reminder, goal pursuit: efficacy/consequences/feedback
intervention_description: Participants received 2–3 health-focused SMS per week for eight consecutive weeks via Textedly. The intervention contained messages describing health benefits of plant-based diets, potential health consequences of animal-based diets, practical strategies to increase plant-based eating (including links to supportive websites in each message), and information about the increasing popularity of PBD among Americans.

outcome_description: Meat servings consumed during the previous day

paper_authors: Hankins, E.; Balhatchet, C.; Bryant, C.; Wilks, M.; Macdonald, C.; Hankins, R.; Abugu, P.; Walker Mackay, T.; McFarlane Smith, W.; Hazel, H.-W.; Clargo, S.
paper_title: Educational presentations reduce meat consumption across two universities
intervention_description: Participants received the Educa­ted Choices Program’s (ECP) The Planet and Our Plates presentation, which describes the environmental impacts of animal agriculture versus plant-based food choices. For example, viewers learn about the land use of animal agriculture versus growing food for direct human consumption, feed-conversion ratios, the environmental consequences of aquaculture (e.g. ocean acidification), emissions from concentrated animal feeding operations (CAFOs), and more. Afterward, all participants received a free food voucher for a local café.  Café  staff, unaware of the video conditions, recorded whether the voucher was used for a vegetarian/vegan (veg*n) meal or a meal that contains meat.
intervention_sample_description: Edinburgh residents recruited via the university and social media
control_description: Participants received the Patagonia Films’ We the Power: The Future of Energy is Community-Owned (Patagonia, 2021), a short film documenting community efforts to produce and have autonomy over their own renewable energies rather than rely on large energy sectors. Afterward, all participants received a  free  food  voucher  for  a  local  café.  Café  staff,  unaware  of  the  video  conditions,  recorded  whether the voucher was used for a vegetarian/vegan (veg*n) meal or a meal that contains meat.
control_sample_description: Edinburgh residents recruited via the university and social media
outcome_description: Percentage of participants choosing to redeem a vegetarian or vegan food item with the voucher

paper_authors: Hankins, E.; Balhatchet, C.; Bryant, C.; Wilks, M.; Macdonald, C.; Hankins, R.; Abugu, P.; Walker Mackay, T.; McFarlane Smith, W.; Hazel, H.-W.; Clargo, S.
paper_title: Educational presentations reduce meat consumption across two universities
intervention_description: Participants received the Educa­ted Choices Program’s (ECP) The Planet and Our Plates presentation, which describes the environmental impacts of animal agriculture versus plant-based food choices. For example, viewers learn about the land use of animal agriculture versus growing food for direct human consumption, feed-conversion ratios, the environmental consequences of aquaculture (e.g. ocean acidification), emissions from concentrated animal feeding operations (CAFOs), and more.  In  exchange for watching this presentation - video, participants received a free ticket to a formal university dinner, pre-selecting either an  "omnivorous" or "plant-based" meal.
intervention_sample_description: students from five University of Cambridge colleges
control_description: Participants received the Patagonia Films’ We the Power: The Future of Energy is Community-Owned (Patagonia, 2021), a short film documenting community efforts to produce and have autonomy over their own renewable energies rather than rely on large energy sectors.   In  exchange  for  watching  this film,  participants  received  a  free  ticket  to  a  formal  university  dinner,  pre-selecting  either  an  "omnivorous"  or  "plant-based"  meal.
control_sample_description: students from five University of Cambridge colleges
outcome_description: Percentage of participants choosing the plant-based meal

paper_authors: Hankins, E.; Balhatchet, C.; Bryant, C.; Wilks, M.; Macdonald, C.; Hankins, R.; Abugu, P.; Walker Mackay, T.; McFarlane Smith, W.; Hazel, H.-W.; Clargo, S.
paper_title: Educational presentations reduce meat consumption across two universities
intervention_description: Participants received the ECP’s Food for Thought presentation, which critically examines the ethical impacts of modern animal agriculture. The presentation highlights animal welfare concerns as well as the significant physical and psychological occupational hazards faced by workers, demonstrating how consumer demand drives these issues. In exchange for watching this presentation - video,  participants received a free ticket to a formal university dinner, pre-selecting either an "omnivorous" or "plant-based" meal.
intervention_sample_description: students from five University of Cambridge colleges
control_description: Participants received the Patagonia Films’ We the Power: The Future of Energy is Community-Owned (Patagonia, 2021), a short film documenting community efforts to produce and have autonomy over their own renewable energies rather than rely on large energy sectors.   In  exchange  for  watching  this film,  participants  received  a  free  ticket  to  a  formal  university  dinner,  pre-selecting  either  an  "omnivorous"  or  "plant-based"  meal.
control_sample_description: students from five University of Cambridge colleges
outcome_description: Percentage of participants choosing the plant-based meal

paper_authors: Reinders, M. J.; Huitink, M.; Dijkstra, S. C.; Maaskant, A. J.; Heijnen, J.
paper_title: Menu-engineering in restaurants - Adapting portion sizes on plates to enhance vegetable consumption: A real-life experiment
paper_source: International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity
intervention_description: The intervention was implemented in three restaurant locations of a major restaurant chain in medium-sized cities in the Netherlands. The portion sizes of vegetables on the plates of main dishes were doubled (150 g of vegetables instead of 75 g) and the portion sizes of meat were reduced by an average of 12.5%. During the intervention, restaurants were phoned weekly by the researcher to discuss the progress of the intervention.
intervention_sample_description: patrons at one of the three participating restaurant locations of a major restaurant chain in the Netherlands
control_description: The intervention was implemented in three restaurant locations of a major restaurant chain in medium-sized cities in the Netherlands. The portion sizes of the main dishes remained as usual.
control_sample_description: patrons at one of the three participating restaurant locations of a major restaurant chain in the Netherlands
outcome_description: Amount of meat/fish consumed in total

paper_authors: Wolfson, J. A.; Musicus, A. A.; Leung, C. W.; Gearhardt, A. N.; Falbe, J.
paper_title: Effect of climate change impact menu labels on fast food ordering choices among US adults: A randomized clinical trial.
intervention_description: Participants were shown a fast food restaurant menu with green low–climate impact labels on chicken, fish, or vegetarian items (positive framing); The top of each menu featured a disclosure statement explaining the label’s meaning: “This item is environmentally sustainable. It has low greenhouse gas emissions and a low contribution to climate change.”
intervention_sample_description: respondents recruited from the National Opinion Research Center (NORC) AmeriSpeak panel
control_description: Participants were shown a fast food restaurant menu with QR code labels on all items.
control_sample_description: respondents recruited from the National Opinion Research Center (NORC) AmeriSpeak panel
outcome_description: Predicted probability of selecting the Impossible burger (which is the plant-based burger)

paper_authors: Wolfson, J. A.; Musicus, A. A.; Leung, C. W.; Gearhardt, A. N.; Falbe, J.
paper_title: Effect of climate change impact menu labels on fast food ordering choices among US adults: A randomized clinical trial.
intervention_description: Participants were shown a fast food restaurant menu with red high–climate impact labels on beef items (negative framing); “This item is not environmentally sustainable. It has high greenhouse gas emissions and a high contribution to climate change”
intervention_sample_description: respondents recruited from the National Opinion Research Center (NORC) AmeriSpeak panel
control_description: Participants were shown a fast food restaurant menu with QR code labels on all items.
control_sample_description: respondents recruited from the National Opinion Research Center (NORC) AmeriSpeak panel
outcome_description: Predicted probability of selecting the Impossible burger (which is the plant-based burger)

paper_authors: Krpan, D.; Houtsma, N.
paper_title: To veg or not to veg? The impact of framing on vegetarian food choice
intervention_description: Participants were shown a restaurant menu in which two vegetarian dishes are listed with a title highlighting environmental benefits of vegetarian food: "Environmentally Friendly courses for a happy planet". For some participants this section was on the top of menu, whereas it was on the down for others.
intervention_sample_description: Participants living in US obtained through Amazon MTurk with a compensation of  $0.60
control_description: Participants were shown a restaurant menu in which two vegetarian dishes are listed under the title of vegetarian, this section was either at the top or down of the menu.
control_sample_description: Participants living in US obtained through Amazon MTurk with a compensation of  $0.60
outcome_description: Percentage of participants who selected a vegetarian main course

paper_authors: Parkin, B.L.; Attwood, S.
paper_title: Menu design approaches to promote sustainable vegetarian food choices when dining out
intervention_description: The vegetarian and meat items were presented in fixed positions across all menus. In this condition, vegetarian dishes were presented without any V symbol this time.
intervention_sample_description: UK Prolific workers with sampling quotas set for age and gender
control_description: The vegetarian and meat items were presented in fixed positions across all menus. In this condition, vegetarian dishes were presented with vegetarian symbol (V) on the left side of the dish name.
control_sample_description: UK Prolific workers with sampling quotas set for age and gender
outcome_description: Number of participants choosing the meat option from the menu

paper_authors: Parkin, B.L.; Attwood, S.

control_sample_description: UK Prolific workers with sampling quotas set for age and gender
outcome_description: Number of participants choosing the meat option from the menu

paper_authors: Hielkema, M. H.; Lund, T. B.
paper_title: A “vegetarian curry stew” or just a “curry stew”? - The effect of neutral labeling of vegetarian dishes on food choice among meat-reducers and non-reducers
intervention_description: For all the conditions, the targeted dish was always listed as the third option to avoid a placement effect. The target dish had a neutral label "Curry stew with coconut and sweet potatoes*", with a footnote mentioning that it's also suitable for vegetarians. The dish was not labeled as vegetarian, vegan, plant-based, or meat-free, as in the other conditions
intervention_sample_description: respondents recruited from an online panel
control_description: For all the conditions, the targeted dish was always listed as the third option to avoid a placement effect. The target dish had an explicit vegan label "Vegan curry stew with coconut and sweet potatoes"
control_sample_description: respondents recruited from an online panel
outcome_description: Percentage of participants choosing a meat dish (chicken curry, red prawn curry, fried noodles with beef) from the menu

paper_authors: Hielkema, M. H.; Lund, T. B.
paper_title: A “vegetarian curry stew” or just a “curry stew”? - The effect of neutral labeling of vegetarian dishes on food choice among meat-reducers and non-reducers
intervention_description: For all the conditions, the targeted dish was always listed as the third option to avoid a placement effect. intervention_sample_description: respondents recruited from an online panel
control_description: For all the conditions, the targeted dish was always listed as the third option to avoid a placement effect. The target dish had an explicit vegetarian label "Vegetarian curry stew with coconut and sweet potatoes"
control_sample_description: respondents recruited from an online panel
outcome_description: Percentage of participants choosing a meat dish (chicken curry, red prawn curry, fried noodles with beef) from the menu

paper_authors: Shreedhar, G.; Galizzi, M. M.
paper_title: Personal or planetary health? Direct, spillover and carryover effects of non-monetary benefits of vegetarian behaviour
intervention_description: Participants were exposed to an implementation intentions intervention that frames the benefits of plant-based foods in terms of personal health. Afterwards, they continue with the food choice task, after which they immediately face the charitable donation task.
intervention_sample_description: UK Prolific workers
control_description: Participants were directly presented with the food choice task without any intervention.
control_sample_description: UK Prolific workers
outcome_description: Proportion of participants who chose a vegetarian option

outcome_description: Proportion of participants who chose a vegetarian option

paper_authors: Bouwman, E. P.; Bolderdijk, J. W.; Onwezen, M. C.; Taufik, D.
paper_title: "Do you consider animal welfare to be important?" Activating cognitive dissonance via value activation can promote vegetarian choices
intervention_description: Participants were presented with an image of a zoo or hospital accompanied by a text asking them to imagine being at the zoo/hospital, "During your visit you get hungry and decide to have lunch in the restaurant of the zoo/hospital.” followed by a question of whether they consider animal welfare to be important.
intervention_sample_description: Participants were recruited by a professional market research company to be representative of the Dutch population based on sex, age, education and region
control_description: Participants were presented with an image of a zoo or hospital accompanied by a text asking them to imagine being at the zoo/hospital, "During your visit you get hungry and decide to have lunch in the restaurant of the zoo/hospital.” and no question afterwards.
control_sample_description: Participants were recruited by a professional market research company to be representative of the Dutch population based on sex, age, education and region
outcome_label: intention to eat a vegetarian lunch
outcome_description: Intention to choose a vegetarian lunch after reading the hospital/zoo scenario

paper_authors: Bouwman, E. P.; Bolderdijk, J. W.; Onwezen, M. C.; Taufik, D.
paper_title: "Do you consider animal welfare to be important?" Activating cognitive dissonance via value activation can promote vegetarian choices
intervention_description: Participants were presented with an image of a zoo or hospital accompanied by a text asking them to imagine being at the zoo/hospital, "During your visit you get hungry and decide to have lunch in the restaurant of the zoo/hospital.” followed by a question of whether they consider health to be important.
intervention_sample_description: Participants were recruited by a professional market research company to be representative of the Dutch population based on sex, age, education and region
control_description: Participants were presented with an image of a zoo or hospital accompanied by a text asking them to imagine being at the zoo/hospital, "During your visit you get hungry and decide to have lunch in the restaurant of the zoo/hospital.” and no question afterwards.
control_sample_description: Participants were recruited by a professional market research company to be representative of the Dutch population based on sex, age, education and region
outcome_label: intention to eat a vegetarian lunch
outcome_description: Intention to choose a vegetarian lunch after reading the hospital/zoo scenario

paper_authors: Bouwman, E. P.; Bolderdijk, J. W.; Onwezen, M. C.; Taufik, D.
paper_title: "Do you consider animal welfare to be important?" Activating cognitive dissonance via value activation can promote vegetarian choices
intervention_description: Participants saw banners and poster in the restaurant promoting vegetarian burgers with “Do you consider animal welfare to be important?“
intervention_sample_description: Customers that entered the restaurant in the Dutch zoo "Ouwehands Dierenpark" between September and October 2022
control_description: Participants didn't see any promotional material in the restaurant for the vegetarian burgers.

paper_authors: Flynn, M. M.; Reinert, S. E.; Schiff, A. R.
paper_title: A six-week cooking program of plant-based recipes improves food security, body weight, and food purchases for food pantry clients
intervention_mechanism: info: health, goal pursuit: cooking skills
intervention_description: Participants participated in a 6-week cooking class in which they observed the preparation of a plant-based, olive oil diet meal and got to taste test; While the recipe was being prepared, the staff discussed consistent themes each week, including the health benefits of extra virgin olive oil and encouraging the participants to use olive oil to cook their vegetables; meat/poultry/seafood is not needed daily; vegetables and all starch products (including beans) contain protein. No other nutrition- or food-related information was provided. All of the recipes were made with extra virgin olive oil (1 to 2 tablespoons), contained on average 2 servings of vegetables, and did not contain meat/poultry or seafood.
intervention_sample_description: food pantry clients
control_sample_description: food pantry clients
outcome_description: Number of reported meals per week that were plant based recipes using olive oil for cooking and did not contain meat/poultry/seafood

paper_authors: Hansen, P. G.; Schilling, M.; Malthesen, M. S.
paper_title: Nudging healthy and sustainable food choices: Three randomized controlled field experiments using a vegetarian lunch-default as a normative signal
intervention_description: Participants received a registration presenting a vegetarian buffet as the default, allowing the active choice of a non-vegetarian option. They saw the following text: "At the conference a vegetarian buffet will be served for lunch. Please state here if you would like to have a non-vegetarian dish prepared for you"
intervention_sample_description: politicians, decision makers, organizations, and staff representatives within public health who attended the 'Citizens expectations about and behaviour relative to the national health services' conference
control_description: Participants received a standard lunch registration presenting a non-vegetarian buffet as the default, but allowing the active choice of a vegetarian option. They saw the following text: "At the conference a non-vegetarian buffet will be served for lunch. Please state here if you would like to have a vegetarian dish prepared for you"
control_sample_description: politicians, decision makers, organizations, and staff representatives within public health who attended the 'Citizens expectations about and behaviour relative to the national health services' conference
outcome_description: Percentage of participants choosing the vegetarian option for lunch

paper_authors: Scharmer, A.
paper_title: For the planet, the animals, or oneself? Measuring individuals’ motivations to restrict meat consumption and encouraging dietary change
intervention_description: The intervention included a message-matching procedure in which individuals view a randomly assigned persuasive appeal related to one of the four previously identified motives. Participants were presented with a 120-word message designed to encompass four distinct motivational factors. In this condition, the message is specifically framed around the theme of animal welfare. (e.g. positive outcomes for animal welfare)
intervention_sample_description: Participants all resided in the United States and in response to a screening question about dietary restrictions, they did not indicate that they followed a vegan or vegetarian diet
control_sample_description: Participants all resided in the United States and in response to a screening question about dietary restrictions, they did not indicate that they followed a vegan or vegetarian diet
outcome_description: Participants answered a single item about their intentions to reduce their meat consumption

paper_authors: Carfora, V.; Catellani, P.
paper_title: Legumes or meat? The effectiveness of recommendation messages towards a plant-based diet depends on people’s identification with flexitarians
intervention_description: Every day at 9:30 and for 14 days (between T1 and T2), participants received persuasive messages via PsyMe app. These messages focused on the lower environmental impact of cultivating legumes than producing meat (e.g., “Compared to livestock feeding production, legume cultivation requires a limited use of fertilizers. Thus, it has a lower pollution impact. If you eat legumes rather than meat, you protect the planet from pollution”).
intervention_sample_description: Italian adults recruited by the university app
control_description: Participants did not receive any messages.
control_sample_description: Italian adults recruited by the university app
outcome_description: Consumption of red, processed, and white meat over the previous week

paper_authors: Carfora, V.; Catellani, P.
paper_title: Legumes or meat? The effectiveness of recommendation messages towards a plant-based diet depends on people’s identification with flexitarians
intervention_description: Every day at 9:30 and for 14 days (between T1 and T2), participants received replacement messages combined with a reference to the dynamic norm (e.g., “Nowadays, more and more people eat legumes instead of meat. Compared to livestock feeding production, legume cultivation requires limited use of fertilizers. Thus, it has a low pollution impact. If you also eat legumes instead of meat, you protect the planet from pollution”).
intervention_sample_description: Italian adults recruited by the university app
control_description: Participants did not receive any messages.
control_sample_description: Italian adults recruited by the university app
outcome_description: Consumption of red, processed, and white meat over the previous week

paper_authors: Nykänen, E. P.; Hoppu, U.; Löyttyniemi, E.; Sandell, M.
paper_title: Nudging Finnish adults into replacing red meat with plant-based protein via presenting foods as dish of the day and altering the dish sequence
intervention_description: Participants received a menu that promoted the vegetarian dish as the Dish of the Day. The meat dish was served before the vegetarian dish in the buffet line at the moment of entry.
intervention_sample_description: Finnish-speaking persons aged 18–65 whose diet contained meat
control_description: Participants received a standard menu, and the meat dish was served before the vegetarian dish at the buffet line at the moment of entry.
control_sample_description: Finnish-speaking persons aged 18–65 whose diet contained meat
outcome_description: Mean share of MMC in the meal weight

paper_authors: Vandenbroele, J.; Slabbinck, H.; Van Kerckhove, A.; Vermeir, I.
paper_title: Mock meat in the butchery: Nudging consumers toward meat substitutes
paper_source: Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes
intervention_description: Two sandwiches were target products in a large scale retail store: one containing meat (i.e., chicken curry salad) and one meat-free (i.e., vegetarian curry salad). The vegetarian curry salad was offered on a separate, vegetarian shelf in the vegetables and fruits department, as before, but also next to the meat version in the butchery department.
intervention_sample_description: data points represent number of target products sold
control_description: Two sandwiches were target products in a large scale retail store: one containing meat (i.e., chicken curry salad) and one meat-free (i.e., vegetarian curry salad). The vegetarian curry salad was only offered on a separate, vegetarian shelf in the vegetables and fruits department.
control_sample_description: data points represent number of target products sold
outcome_description: Percentage of curry salad sales that contained meat (vs. no meat)

paper_authors: Vandenbroele, J.; Slabbinck, H.; Van Kerckhove, A.; Vermeir, I.
paper_title: Mock meat in the butchery: Nudging consumers toward meat substitutes
paper_source: Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes
intervention_description: Eight sandwich spreads were the target products, representing four pairs of a meat product and meat substitute: (1) “martino” (i.e., spicy minced meat) and vegetarian “martino”, (2) thin chicken slices with herbs versus vegetarian baloney slices with tomato and basil, (3) chicken curry salad and vegetarian curry salad, and (4) baloney slices versus vegetarian baloney slices. The sandwich department in the hypothetical retail store, established in a university lab, consisted of two refrigerators, placed back-to-back; only one was immediately visible to the participants as they entered the lab. In each condition, an equal number of products appeared in both fridges (i.e., four products each), but we provided multiple packages of the same product to make the store appear realistic. For this condition, the fridge with the sandwich spreads was not immediately visible to participants. The vegetarian spreads were placed directly next to the meat-based spreads.
intervention_sample_description: data points represent number of products selected
control_description: Eight sandwich spreads were the target products, representing four pairs of a meat product and meat substitute: (1) “martino” (i.e., spicy minced meat) and vegetarian “martino”, (2) thin chicken slices with herbs versus vegetarian baloney slices with tomato and basil, (3) chicken curry salad and vegetarian curry salad, and (4) baloney slices versus vegetarian baloney slices. The sandwich department in the hypothetical retail store, established in a university lab, consisted of two refrigerators, placed back-to-back; only one was immediately visible to the participants as they entered the lab. In each condition, an equal number of products appeared in both fridges (i.e., four products each), but we provided multiple packages of the same product to make the store appear realistic. For this condition, the fridge with the sandwich spreads was not immediately visible to participants. The vegetarian spreads were placed seperate from the meat-based spreads.
control_sample_description: data points represent number of products selected
outcome_description: Percentage of sandwich spreads that contained meat (vs. no meat)

paper_authors: Piernas, C.; Cook, B.; Stevens, R.; Stewart, C.; Hollowell, J.; Scarborough, P.; Jebb, S. A.
paper_title: Estimating the effect of moving meat-free products to the meat aisle on sales of meat and meat-free products: A non-randomised controlled intervention study in a large UK supermarket chain
intervention_description: The intervention was implemented in 20 stores on the week commencing January 27, 2019, for 12 weeks. The intervention consisted of moving 26 uniquely barcoded meat-free products within 4 meat-free categories (50% sausages, 30% burgers, 12% meatballs, and 8% mince) from their usual section into a newly created meat-free bay within the meat aisle. This new bay of meatfree products covered the whole bay, including top, middle, and bottom shelves. This meat free section replaced a bay where either meat was previously sold (16 stores) or where the bay previously contained chilled condiments (3 stores) or was unused (1 store)
intervention_sample_description: Weekly sales data were obtained from 108 stores: 20 intervention stores that moved a selection of 26 meat-free products into a newly created meat free bay within the meat aisle and 88 matched control stores.
control_description: In the control stores, meat-free products remained where originally located (usually in their own meat-free section outside the meat aisle) without changes to their position
control_sample_description: Weekly sales data were obtained from 108 stores: 20 intervention stores that moved a selection of 26 meat-free products into a newly created meat free bay within the meat aisle and 88 matched control stores.
outcome_description: changes in (units/items sold) per store per week for intervention and control conditions

paper_authors: Kanchanachitra, M.; Chamchan, C.; Kanchanachitra, C.; Suttikasem, K.; Gunn, L. H.; Vlaev, I.
paper_title: Nudge interventions to reduce fish sauce consumption in Thailand
intervention_description: After buying their noodles, consumers can add fish sauce. The fish sauce serving container was changed to a bowl (with 500g of fish sauce) with a “regular spoon” (that can extract 15g of fish sauce), instead of being served in the 750ml bottle
intervention_sample_description: datapoint represents number of bowls sold at the five canteens in Thailand
control_description: In the control scenario, fish sauce was served in a 750 ml bottle, placed in front of each noodle shop. After the consumers buy their noodles, they can choose if they want to add any condiments to their desired taste. After buying their noodles, consumers can add fish sauce, served in the 750ml bottle (standard noodle shop practice of serving fish sauce)
control_sample_description: datapoint represents number of bowls sold at the five canteens in Thailand
outcome_label: fish sauce used per bowl sold per day
outcome_description: Amount of fish sauce used (in grams) out of the number of bowls sold

paper_authors: Kanchanachitra, M.; Chamchan, C.; Kanchanachitra, C.; Suttikasem, K.; Gunn, L. H.; Vlaev, I.
paper_title: Nudge interventions to reduce fish sauce consumption in Thailand
intervention_description: After buying their noodles, consumers can add fish sauce. The fish sauce serving container was changed to a bowl (with 500g of fish sauce) with a “special spoon” (that can hold 3.3g of fish sauce), instead of being served in the 750ml bottle. The special spoon, named the “less spoon”, has a hole in the middle and can extract 3.3 g of fish sauce at a time, or approximately two thirds of a teaspoon. Displayed near the bowl of fish sauce was an A4-sized information sheet introducing the “less spoon” with the short message: “Do not add more than 2/3 teaspoons of fish sauce per meal”.
intervention_sample_description: datapoint represents number of bowls sold at the five canteens in Thailand
control_description: In the control scenario, fish sauce was served in a 750 ml bottle, placed in front of each noodle shop. After the consumers buy their noodles, they can choose if they want to add any condiments to their desired taste. After buying their noodles, consumers can add fish sauce, served in the 750ml bottle (standard noodle shop practice of serving fish sauce)
control_sample_description: datapoint represents number of bowls sold at the five canteens in Thailand
outcome_label: fish sauce used per bowl sold per day
outcome_description: Amount of fish sauce used (in grams) out of the number of bowls sold

paper_authors: Kanchanachitra, M.; Chamchan, C.; Kanchanachitra, C.; Suttikasem, K.; Gunn, L. H.; Vlaev, I.
paper_title: Nudge interventions to reduce fish sauce consumption in Thailand
intervention_description: After buying their noodles, consumers can add fish sauce. The fish sauce was served in a bottle, but an A4-sized information sheet was also displayed near the fish sauce bottle. The sheet provided information regarding the amount of sodium already in noodles and the recommended sodium consumption per meal. The information sheet also showed a picture of a smartwatch with a healthy heart, a 120/80 blood pressure reading, a positive reinforcement message “GREAT JOB”, and a short message reading: “Less salty, reduces disease, weight, and bloating”.
intervention_sample_description: datapoint represents number of bowls sold at the five canteens in Thailand
control_description: In the control scenario, fish sauce was served in a 750 ml bottle, placed in front of each noodle shop. After the consumers buy their noodles, they can choose if they want to add any condiments to their desired taste. After buying their noodles, consumers can add fish sauce, served in the 750ml bottle (standard noodle shop practice of serving fish sauce)
control_sample_description: datapoint represents number of bowls sold at the five canteens in Thailand
outcome_label: fish sauce used per bowl sold per day
outcome_description: Amount of fish sauce used (in grams) out of the number of bowls sold

In addition, there was a picture of a girl making a disgusted face with a short message saying “too salty”.

intervention_description: Participants watched the documentary Cowspiracy, which addresses the environmental impact of meat production.
intervention_sample_description: Young adults who consumed meat almost on a daily basis recruited via flyers, social media, and e-mail
control_description: Participants watched the nature documentary, “Planet Earth” (2006) about environmental change, where no reference to meat consumption was made.

paper_authors: Egeler, G.A.; Baur, P.
paper_title: Menu choice and meat-eating habits: Results of a field experiment in two university canteens
intervention_description: In both the control and the interventions, the vegetarian menu line was abolished, meat and veg-dishes were integrated into all three menu lines, veg-dishes were not labelled or marketed as such. The share of veg-dishes was increased and only 33% of the meals offered in the cafeteria contained meat.
intervention_sample_description: canteen visitors who bought a meal at the target university canteens
control_description: In both the control and the interventions, the vegetarian menu line was abolished, meat and veg-dishes were integrated into all three menu lines, veg-dishes were not labelled or marketed as such. 64% of the meals offered in the cafeteria contained meat (close to the number of meat meals offered in previous years).
control_sample_description: canteen visitors who bought a meal at the target university canteens
outcome_description: Percentage of vegetarian dishes sold

paper_authors: Egeler, G.A.; Baur, P.

control_sample_description: canteen visitors who bought a meal at the target university canteens

paper_authors: Hallez, L.; Qutteina, Y.; Boen, F.; Smits, T.
paper_title: The ABC’s of ecological and nutrition labels. The impact of label theme and complexity on the environmental footprint of online grocery choices
intervention_description: Participants had to choose from products that were presented with an eco-score label, which evaluated the overall environmental impact of the products with a letter from A to E and a color from green to red, to make a meal.
intervention_sample_description: Undergraduate students at a Flemish university
control_description: Participants had to choose from products that were presented without any labels to make a meal. They received the following instructions: ‘We would like you to imagine that you are shopping online for ingredients to make a pasta dish for yourself (1 person). You can compose this meal as you like. You can add any ingredient to your shopping list, and you can select as many ingredients as you like’. A pasta dish was chosen given the many possible varieties. The shop included 30 food products from four categories, i.e., proteins (e.g., meat, fish, vegetarian meat), vegetables, pasta, and sauce. The category order was randomized between participants.
control_sample_description: Undergraduate students at a Flemish university
outcome_description: Amount of meat included in the chosen ingredients

paper_title: The effect of normative conflict on meat consumption behavior: A mixed-method study
intervention_description: Participants read a text that asked them to imagine a situation in which there was a conflict between the social norms of close family (pro meat) and a relevant friend group (anti meat). The situation was a lunch commemorating the participant's birthday, and the menu offered was beef barbecue and various types of vegetables roasted on the grill.
intervention_sample_description: adults recruited via the researcher's social networks
control_description: In the control condition, participants were asked to read a 172-word text involving the same situation (a birthday meal and groups of family and friends) but without referencing social norms related to meat consumption.
control_sample_description: adults recruited via the researcher's social networks
outcome_description: Participants' intention to choose the meat in their imagined scenario

outcome_description: Percentage of vegetarian dishes selected

paper_authors: Blondin, S.; Attwood, S.; Vennard, D.; Mayneris, V.
paper_title: Environmental messages promote plant-based food choices: An online restaurant menu study
intervention_description: Participants received the following message before making a dish selection: "Choosing plant-based food is the best way to feed a growing population while protecting our planet’s climate for generations to come. A greener future for our children starts on your plate."
intervention_sample_description: US Prolific workers
control_description: Participants didn't receive a text message.
control_sample_description: US Prolific workers
outcome_description: Percentage of vegetarian dishes selected

paper_authors: Çoker, E. N.; Pechey, R.; Jebb, S. A.
paper_title: Testing the effect of a dynamic descriptive social norm message on meat-free food selection in worksite cafeterias: A randomized controlled trial
intervention_description: Large free-standing banners promoting the intervention message (“More and more of your [company name] colleagues are choosing veggie options, join them today!") were placed at the entrance of the cafeteria. Floor stickers that guided the customers from the entrance to the hot main meal buffet were placed in the dining area, A4-sized posters were placed next to or above the buffet, and star stickers that were placed next to the vegetarian options on the menu ("Spotted the star? Look for the star on our most loved veggie options”)
intervention_sample_description: datapoints represent worksite cafeterias in the UK
control_sample_description: datapoints represent worksite cafeterias in the UK
outcome_label: weekly percentage of meat-free meal sales trial
outcome_description: Percentage of weekly meat-free meal sales

paper_authors: Marty, L.; Biehlmann, M.; Louveau, A.; Poquet, D.; Robinson, E.
paper_title: The “Eat Less Meat” one-month challenge: a randomized controlled trial of a meat reduction pledge intervention among French university students
paper_source: International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity
intervention_mechanism: goal pursuit: cooking skills, goal pursuit: efficacy/consequences/feedback, info: health, info: environment, info: animal welfare, goal pursuit: planning/pledge/reminder
intervention_description: Participants received by email a cookbook of ten easy-to-prepare and cheap meat-free recipes and a starter kit with advice and motivational tips. For a month, they received an email each week (four in total) with more advice and motivational tips. They were also asked to follow the Instagram account of the “Eat less meat” one-month challenge, where during one month, one publication was posted every day except on Sundays. Engaging stories requiring interactions were posted regularly, participants could ask their questions to the research team, and participants could communicate. The online materials targeted both reflective motivation (directly) by attempting to enhance health, environment and animal welfare concerns and automatic motivation (indirectly through capability and opportunity) by attempting to alleviate barriers to reduce meat consumption and thus increasing participants’ commitment to the challenge.
intervention_sample_description: university students who are registered in a Bourgogne-Franche-Comté university, fluent in French, had an email address and Instagram account, were not pescatarian, vegetarian nor vegan, were not pregnant or breastfeeding, and did not have iron deficiency or a body mass index below 18.5
control_description: Participants in the control group took part in the challenge three months after the participants in the intervention group (wait list control group).
control_sample_description: university students who are registered in a Bourgogne-Franche-Comté university, fluent in French, had an email address and Instagram account, were not pescatarian, vegetarian nor vegan, were not pregnant or breastfeeding, and did not have iron deficiency or a body mass index below 18.5
outcome_description: Daily meat intake in grams (i.e., beef patties, beef, pork, veal, lamb, poultry, liver, other offal, salami, mortadella, ham and sausages)

paper_authors: Abrahamse, W.; Steg, L.; Vlek, C.; Rothengatter, T.
paper_title: The effect of tailored information, goal setting, and tailored feedback on household energy use, energy-related behaviors, and behavioral antecedents
intervention_description: Two groups of households received tailored energy-saving information, including details about global warming and energy costs, plus a personalized list of relevant energy-saving measures. Both groups were asked to achieve a 5% energy reduction and received individual feedback via a website showing their percentage energy savings, the contribution of each behavior, and money saved. The first group received only individual goals and feedback, while the second group also received a collective 5% group goal and monthly feedback about the group's average and total energy savings via newsletter.
intervention_sample_description: households from Groningen, the Netherlands
control_description: Households in the control group did not receive any information about energy problems, they were not given any tailored information, nor were they asked to reduce their energy use by 5%. They did not receive any feedback either.
control_sample_description: households from Groningen, the Netherlands
outcome_description: Meat consumption per meal

paper_authors: Acharya, S.; Maskarinec, G.; Williams, A. E.; Oshiro, C.; Hebshi, S.; Murphy, P.
paper_title: Nutritional changes among premenopausal women undertaking a soya based dietary intervention study in Hawaii
paper_source: Journal of Human Nutrition and Dietetics
intervention_mechanism: goal pursuit: food provision, info: environment, goal pursuit: planning/pledge/reminder
intervention_description: Regular supplies of soy foods were given free-of-charge to the intervention group. In both the intervention and control condition, the study dietitian made speciﬁc suggestions on how to incorporate soy foods into the daily menu, such as replacing meat with tofu in stew or fried dishes, cow milk with soyamilk, and cookies with a soya protein bar. To avoid weight gain, replacing other foods was emphasized over adding soya foods. To maintain compliance, all participants received individual dietary counselling by a registered dietitian and follow-up phone calls. They had at least six contacts with the dietitian during the ﬁrst year and were counselled on how to maintain a healthy diet according to the food guide pyramid.
intervention_sample_description: Women between 35 and 46 years with low soya intake, a normal mammogram, regular menstrual cycles, and an intact uterus, from Hawaii.
control_description: In both the intervention and control condition, the study dietitian made speciﬁc suggestions on how to incorporate soy foods into the daily menu, such as replacing meat with tofu in stew or fried dishes, cow milk with soyamilk, and cookies with a soya protein bar. To avoid weight gain, replacing other foods was emphasized over adding soya foods. To maintain compliance, all participants received individual dietary counselling by a registered dietitian and follow-up phone calls. They had at least six contacts with the dietitian during the ﬁrst year and were counselled on how to maintain a healthy diet according to the food guide pyramid.
control_sample_description: Women between 35 and 46 years with low soya intake, a normal mammogram, regular menstrual cycles, and an intact uterus, from Hawaii.
outcome_description: Total dairy servings consumed during the previous day

paper_authors: Aldoh, A.; Sparks, P.; Harris, P. R.
paper_title: Dynamic norms and food choice: Reflections on a failure of minority norm information to influence motivation to reduce meat consumption
intervention_description: Participants read a norm statement depicting a dynamic norm regarding meat consumption in the UK: "Recent research has shown that, in the last 5 years, 30% of people in the UK have now started to make an effort to limit their meat consumption. That means that, in recent years, 3 in 10 people in the UK have begun to eat less meat than they otherwise would. Why do you think this is?"
intervention_sample_description: UK Prolific workers
control_sample_description: UK Prolific workers
outcome_description: Interest in reducing meat consumption (i.e. “How interested are you in eating less meat?”)

paper_authors: Fonseca, R. P.; De Groeve, B.; Graça, J.
paper_title: Masculinizing plant-based diets as an appeal for dietary change among men
intervention_description: Participants saw a social media post in which a masculine model was shown alongside a plant-based meal, associating plant-based dieting with masculinity. The post showed an entrepreneur ostensibly sharing the message: “switching to a plant-based diet was a game changer for my career. #focus #energy # success”.
intervention_sample_description: UK participants from Prolific
control_sample_description: UK participants from Prolific
outcome_description: Participants were asked to rate plant-based diets using four semantic differential items on a 7-point scale: “bad” to “good”, “harmful” to “beneficial”, “unpleasant” to “pleasant”, “unenjoyable” to “enjoyable”

paper_authors: Fonseca, R. P.; De Groeve, B.; Graça, J.

outcome_description: Participants were asked to rate plant-based diets using four semantic differential items on a 7-point scale: “bad” to “good”, “harmful” to “beneficial”, “unpleasant” to “pleasant”, “unenjoyable” to “enjoyable”

paper_title: Masculinizing plant-based diets as an appeal for dietary change among men
intervention_description: Participants saw a social media post in which a masculine model was shown alongside a plant-based meal, associating plant-based dieting with masculinity. The post showed an entrepreneur ostensibly sharing the message: “switching to a plant-based diet was a game changer for my career. #focus #energy # success”.

control_sample_description: UK participants from Prolific
outcome_label: willingness to follow a plant-based diet
outcome_description: Participants rated their willingness to follow a plant-based diet with answer options ranging from 1 “not willing at all” (1) to “very willing” (7).

paper_authors: Pandey, S.; Olsen, A.; Thomsen, M.
paper_title: Nudging strategies to promote plant-based and sustainable food consumption in canteens
intervention_mechanism: info: health, info: environment, choice architecture: visibility/salience/ease
intervention_description: The menus in the canteen were modified by adding plant-based meal alternatives next to meat-based meals, along with priming tools (i.e. messages with each meal, emphasizing the environmental, nutritional and dietary benefits of choosing the plant-based target meal option). These messages were displayed on an A4 size board and placed strategically next to each meal to communicate its climate footprint, dietary recommendations, or nutritional content.
intervention_sample_description: meal purchases at three educational context canteens in Denmark
control_sample_description: meal purchases at three educational context canteens in Denmark
outcome_description: Quantity of meat-based meals in a purchased plate

paper_authors: Dannenberg, A.; Klatt, C.; Weingärtner, E.
paper_title: The effects of social norms and observability on food choice
intervention_description: Participants were provided with details about the university and its student body, including gender distribution, faculty sizes, participation in university sports programs, and the percentage of students studying abroad. Participants were additionally informed of the results from a non-representative survey conducted with a group of enrolled students. Specifically, participants were told that 60% of students in that group reported following a vegetarian or vegan diet. To minimize social desirability bias, participants were explicitly informed that their subsequent voucher choice would remain strictly private.
intervention_sample_description: first-year students at a university in Germany
control_description: No information on dietary habits among the student body was provided. Participants made a private choice of a voucher.
control_sample_description: first-year students at a university in Germany
outcome_description: Percentage of participants choosing the voucher for a meat sandwich

paper_authors: Dannenberg, A.; Klatt, C.; Weingärtner, E.
paper_title: The effects of social norms and observability on food choice
intervention_description: No information on dietary habits among the student body was provided. Participants had to make a public choice of a voucher in front of the group of participants.
intervention_sample_description: first-year students at a university in Germany
control_description: No information on dietary habits among the student body was provided. Participants made a private choice of a voucher.
control_sample_description: first-year students at a university in Germany
outcome_description: Percentage of participants choosing the voucher for a meat sandwich

paper_authors: Dannenberg, A.; Klatt, C.; Weingärtner, E.
