paper_authors: Lemken, D.; Simonetti, A.; Sindermann, G.; Magnasco, A. I. E.
paper_title: Evidence on the effectiveness-acceptance trade-off between forced active choice and default nudging: A field study to reduce meat consumption in cafeterias
intervention_description: Cafeteria users who selected a meat dish were informed that they had the option to choose the same dish with either a small or large meat content, with all other ingredients being adjustable in size. Users were then prompted to make their meal configuration choices. Additionally, signs were placed in the serving area and at the entrance of the dining hall to explain the reduced option. This sign also highlighted with specific numbers how much more people could be nourished if agricultural land were used for bread production instead of pork production.
intervention_sample_description: datapoint represents dish choices made at the cafeteria of a specialized orthopedics health clinic
control_description: The control phase provided a baseline measurement within the cafeteria setting. Reduced meat portions were already an option in the cafeteria, but users were only informed once about the possibility of portion size adjustments upon their arrival at the clinic. This option was not advertised in the cafeteria itself at the time they were making their meal choices
control_sample_description: datapoint represents dish choices made at the cafeteria of a specialized orthopedics health clinic
outcome_description: Percentage of participants choosing the reduced meat portion for their meal

paper_authors: Lemken, D.; Simonetti, A.; Sindermann, G.; Magnasco, A. I. E.
paper_title: Evidence on the effectiveness-acceptance trade-off between forced active choice and default nudging: A field study to reduce meat consumption in cafeterias
intervention_mechanism: choice architecture: availability/variety/size, choice architecture: default
intervention_description: The dish with the reduced meat portion was set as the meat default choice. Consequently, if cafeteria users did not explicitly request the regular meat portion size, they would automatically receive the dish with the smaller meat portion. Signs were placed everywhere that cafeteria users are free to adjust their meat components.
intervention_sample_description: datapoint represents dish choices made at the cafeteria of a specialized orthopedics health clinic
control_description: The control phase provided a baseline measurement within the cafeteria setting. Reduced meat portions were already an option in the cafeteria, but users were only informed once about the possibility of portion size adjustments upon their arrival at the clinic. This option was not advertised in the cafeteria itself at the time they were making their meal choices
control_sample_description: datapoint represents dish choices made at the cafeteria of a specialized orthopedics health clinic
outcome_description: Percentage of participants choosing the reduced meat portion for their meal

paper_authors: Hope, J. E.; Green, S. A.; Peacock, J. R.; Mathur, M.
paper_title: Taking a bite out of meat, or just giving fresh veggies the boot? Plant-based meats did not reduce meat purchasing in a randomized controlled menu intervention
intervention_description: Study topic was introduced as "consumer choices" rather than "meat consumption" to eliminate social desirability effects. Participants were presented with a duplication of the online menu used by the fast casual restaurant Chipotle in the United States. For this condition, sofritas and veggie options were kept, and a fictional PMA designated “chick’nitas” was added, giving participants three non meat options including two PMAs
intervention_sample_description: Adults fluent in English and residing in the U.S recruited via Prolific
control_description: Participants were presented with a duplication of the online menu used by the fast casual restaurant Chipotle in the United States. Participants had the same two non-meat options avaliable to real Chipotle diners.
control_sample_description: Adults fluent in English and residing in the U.S recruited via Prolific
outcome_description: Primary dependent variable was taco filling selection: chicken, steak, beef barbacoa, carnitas, vegetables and guacamole, sofritas, chick’nitas, no protein, or no taco at all.

paper_authors: Schiller, J.; Ruby M. B.; Sproesser, G.

paper_authors: Piazza, J.; McLatchie, N.; Olesen, C.
paper_title: Are baby animals less appetizing? Tenderness toward baby animals and appetite for meat
intervention_description: Participants were presented an image of a cooked meat dish (same for all participants) paired with an image of a calf. The animal was presented above the meat dish on the page and participants were told the meat “comes from baby animal depicted above”.
intervention_sample_description: Amazon MTurk workers based on United States
control_description: Participants were only presented an image of a cooked meat dish without any animal image.
control_sample_description: Amazon MTurk workers based on United States
outcome_description: Participants rated how “appetizing” they found the meat on a sliding scale from 0 (Not at all appetizing) to 100 (Extremely appetizing)

paper_authors: Friis, R.; Skov, L. R.; Olsen, A.; Appleton, K. M.; Saulais, L.; Dinnella, C.; Hartwell, H.; Depezay, L.; Monteleone, E.; Giboreau, A.; Perez-Cueto, F. J. A.
paper_title: Comparison of three nudge interventions (priming, default option, and perceived variety) to promote vegetable consumption in a self-service buffet setting
intervention_description: The food environment was tailored to accommodate a green ambience of plants, green servings bowls and herbs in the dining area. The plants and herbs were placed intentionally, so study participants would be visually exposed when interacting with the entire area. The fresh herbs provided a strong odour and participants could freely use the provided fresh herbs (basil, parsley) as part of their meal.
intervention_sample_description: Participants were recruited via posters, social media, word-of-mouth, and a study volunteer website
control_description: The food environment was not manipulated. The buffet was organised for self-serving, and each dish component was presented in a large bowl (rice, chilli-con-carne and salads) and in a tall pot for the salsa.
control_sample_description: Participants were recruited via posters, social media, word-of-mouth, and a study volunteer website
outcome_label: amount of chili con carne taken
outcome_description: The amont of chili con carne (in grams) that participants took from the buffet.

paper_authors: Friis, R.; Skov, L. R.; Olsen, A.; Appleton, K. M.; Saulais, L.; Dinnella, C.; Hartwell, H.; Depezay, L.; Monteleone, E.; Giboreau, A.; Perez-Cueto, F. J. A.
paper_title: Comparison of three nudge interventions (priming, default option, and perceived variety) to promote vegetable consumption in a self-service buffet setting
intervention_description: The two salads were divided into pre-portioned individual transparent ‘takeaway’ bowls with the default amount of 150g of the white salad and 50g of the red salad. Participants were to take the individual bowls with them, compared to the control situation where they had to serve themselves on their plates. The participants had the possibility of taking as many bowls of salad as they liked.
intervention_sample_description: Participants were recruited via posters, social media, word-of-mouth, and a study volunteer website
control_description: The food environment was not manipulated. The buffet was organised for self-serving, and each dish component was presented in a large bowl (rice, chilli-con-carne and salads) and in a tall pot for the salsa.
control_sample_description: Participants were recruited via posters, social media, word-of-mouth, and a study volunteer website
outcome_label: amount of chili con carne taken
outcome_description: The amont of chili con carne (in grams) that participants took from the buffet.

paper_authors: Friis, R.; Skov, L. R.; Olsen, A.; Appleton, K. M.; Saulais, L.; Dinnella, C.; Hartwell, H.; Depezay, L.; Monteleone, E.; Giboreau, A.; Perez-Cueto, F. J. A.
paper_title: Comparison of three nudge interventions (priming, default option, and perceived variety) to promote vegetable consumption in a self-service buffet setting
intervention_description: The two mixed salads served in the control setting were split into each of the ingredients in separate two-litre square bowls. The overall selection of ingredients was exactly the same, but just divided in to more bowls, hence allowing each participant to compose their own salad.
intervention_sample_description: Participants were recruited via posters, social media, word-of-mouth, and a study volunteer website
control_description: The food environment was not manipulated. The buffet was organised for self-serving, and each dish component was presented in a large bowl (rice, chilli-con-carne and salads) and in a tall pot for the salsa.
control_sample_description: Participants were recruited via posters, social media, word-of-mouth, and a study volunteer website
outcome_label: amount of chili con carne taken
outcome_description: The amont of chili con carne (in grams) that participants took from the buffet.

paper_authors: Murray, S.; Meleady, R., Hodson, G.
paper_title: Seeing animals, choosing plants: Evidence from a cafeteria field study on food choice
intervention_description: During the intervention period, images of animals were added beside the corresponding meat-based meal options on the menus. For instance, an image of a chicken accompanied the sweet and sour chicken, an image of a pig accompanied the pork gyros, and an image of a cow accompanied the beef bolognese. No images were added next to the vegetarian dishes. The images showed the neutral depictions of the animals alone without any other contextual background.
intervention_sample_description: Cafeteria meals purchased at a UK university
control_sample_description: Cafeteria meals purchased at a UK university
outcome_description: The percentange of main meals sold that contained meat.

paper_authors: Denver, S.; Nordström, J.; Christensen, T.
paper_title: Double nudges can accelerate the transition towards more climate-friendly diets – experiment involving default meals and carbon footprint information
intervention_mechanism: choice architecture: visibility/salience/ease, choice architecture: default
intervention_description: Participants' were asked to indicate their preferred protein source for a hypothetical dish and plant-based steak was presented as the default protein. Moreover, when participants ranked their preferred protein sources, plant-based steak was listed at the top.
intervention_sample_description: Danish participants recruited via the panel provider Norstat
control_description: Participants' were asked to indicate their preferred protein source for a hypothetical dish and ground beef was presented as the default protein. Moreover, when participants ranked their preferred protein sources, ground beef was listed at the top.
control_sample_description: Danish participants recruited via the panel provider Norstat
outcome_description: The percentage of participants choosing the plant-based steak for their hypothetical meal, rather than beef, chicken, pork, or fish.

paper_authors: Ardesch, T. F.; Klaperski-van der Wal, S.; Nijssen, S. R. R.; Müller, B. C. N.
paper_title: Reducing meat consumption through default nudging: A field study
intervention_description: The displayed food items in the cafeteria’s showcase (i.e., the defaults) were changed to exclusively feature vegetarian items. Fish and meat items remained available but were listed on screens above the counter and had to be specifically requested; they were no longer displayed in the showcase
intervention_sample_description: Visitors to a university cafeteria at a Dutch univesity
control_sample_description: Visitors to a university cafeteria at a Dutch univesity
outcome_description: The percentage of items sold that contained meat (vs. vegetarian).
